pages.title: Projective duality theorem and finiteness theorem
pages.html:
1 Projective duality theorem and finiteness theorem

The following theorem, essentially contained in FAC1 (apart from the fact that at the time, Serre did not have at his disposal the language of Ext of sheaves of modules), is the global analogue of the local duality theorem (Exposé IV), which was modelled on it.

Theorem 1.1

Let \(k\) be a field, \(X=\mathbf{P}_k^r\) the standard projective scheme of dimension \(r\) over \(k\), \(F\) a variable coherent module on \(X\); then we have an isomorphism of \(\partial\)-functors in \(F\):

\[ \tag{1} {}\operatorname{H}^i(X, F)' \overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \operatorname{Ext}^{r-i}(X;F, \Omega_{X/k}^r), \]

where one sets

\[ \tag{2} {}\Omega_{X/k}^r= \mathscr{O}_{\mathbf{P}_k^r}(-r-1). \]
Remark

Of course, this Module is also the Module of relative differentials of degree \(r\) of \(X\) over \(k\). In this form, the theorem remains true if \(X\) is a proper and smooth scheme over \(k\) (for the projective case, see A. Grothendieck, "Théorèmes de dualité pour les faisceaux algébriques cohérents", Séminaire Bourbaki Mai 1957)2. When \(F\) is locally free, one recovers Serre's duality theorem \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F)' \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{r-i}(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \Omega_{X/k}^r))\). Theorem 1.1 (which moreover recovers the case of an \(X\) projective over \(k\), as in loc. cit. ) will suffice for our purposes.

The homomorphism (1) is deduced from the Yoneda pairing

\[ \tag{3} {}\operatorname{H}^i(X, F) \times \operatorname{Ext}^{r-i}(X;F, \Omega_{X/k}^r) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^r(X, \Omega_{X/k}^r), \]

and from a well-known isomorphism (cf. FAC, or EGA III 2.1.12):

\[ \tag{4} {}\operatorname{H}^r(X, \Omega_{X/k}^r) = \operatorname{H}^r(\mathbf{P}_k^r, \mathscr{O}_{\mathbf{P}_k^r}(-r-1)) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} k. \]

To show that (1) is an isomorphism, one proceeds as in the case of the local duality theorem, noting that \(\operatorname{H}^r(X, F)\) as a functor in \(F\) is right exact (since \(\operatorname{H}^n(X, F)=0\) for \(n>r\)), and that every coherent Module is isomorphic to a quotient of a direct sum of Modules of the form \(\mathscr{O}(-m)\), with \(m\) large. This reduces us, by descending induction on \(i\), to carrying out the verification for a sheaf of the form \(\mathscr{O}(-m)\), where it is contained in the well-known explicit computations (FAC, or EGA III 2.1.12). One may moreover assume \(-m \leqslant -r-1\), in which case \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, \mathscr{O}(-m))=0\) for \(i\neq r\).

Corollary 1.2

For \(F\) coherent given, and \(m\) large enough, we have a canonical isomorphism

\[ \tag{5} {}\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(-m))'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\operatorname{H}^0(X, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^{r-i}(F, \Omega_{X/k}^r)(m)) \]

(where the \('\) denotes the vector-space dual).

Indeed, on a projective scheme \(X\), one has, for every pair of coherent sheaves \(F, G\) and for \(n\) large enough, a canonical isomorphism:

\[ \tag{6} {}\operatorname{Ext}^n(X;F(-m), G)\simeq \operatorname{Ext}^n(X;F, G(m)) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^{n}(F, G)(m)), \]

(the isomorphism of the first two terms being trivially true for every \(m\)), as follows from the spectral sequence of the global \(\operatorname{Ext}\)

\[ \operatorname{H}^p(X, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^q_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G(m))) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}^{\bullet}(X;F, G(m)), \]

which degenerates for \(m\) large enough thanks to the fact that

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^q_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G(m)) \simeq \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^q_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)(m), \]

and that the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^q_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)\) are coherent sheaves. Hence (5) follows from (6) and (1).

Corollary 1.3

Equivalent conditions for \(i, F\) given:

  1. \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(-m))=0\) for \(m\) large.

  2. (i bis)

    \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(\cdot))= \coprod_{m\in\mathbf{Z}}\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(-m))\) is a finitely generated \(S\)-module, where \(S=k[t_o, \ldots, t_r]\).

  3. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{r-i}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \Omega_{X/k}^r)=0\).

  4. (ii bis)

    \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{r-i}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \mathscr{O}_X)=0\).

  5. \(\operatorname{H}^i_x(F_x)=0\) for every closed point \(x\) of \(X\).

  6. \(\operatorname{H}^{i+1}_x(\widetilde{F}_x)=0\) for every closed point \(x\) of the punctured projecting cone \(\widetilde{X}=\operatorname{Spec}(S)-\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) of \(X\), where \(\widetilde{F}\) denotes the inverse image of \(F\) under the canonical morphism \(\widetilde{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\).

Proof▾

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (i bis) since the submodule of \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(\cdot))\) consisting of the sum of the homogeneous components of degree \(\geqslant \nu\) is finitely generated over \(S\) (in fact, for \(i\neq 0\), it is even finitely generated over \(k\)), (cf. FAC or EGA III 2.2.1 and 2.3.2).

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (ii) by virtue of corollary 1.2.

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (ii bis) because \(\Omega_{X/k}^r\) is locally isomorphic to \(\mathscr{O}_X\).

(ii bis) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iii) by virtue of the local duality theorem for \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) (which is a regular local ring of dimension \(r\)), according to which the "dual" of \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{r-i}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \mathscr{O}_X)_x\) is identified with \(\operatorname{H}^i_x(F_x)\) (V 2.1).

(ii bis) is equivalent to the analogous relation

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{r-i}_{\mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{X}}}(\widetilde{F}, \mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{X}})=0 \]

(thanks to the fact that \(\widetilde{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is faithfully flat, so that the inverse image of \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{r-i}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \mathscr{O}_X)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{r-i}_{\mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{X}}}(\widetilde{F}, \mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{X}})\)) and this last relation is equivalent to (iv) by the local duality theorem for the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\), which is regular of dimension \(r+1\).

Q.E.D.

In particular, applying this to all the \(i\leqslant n\), one finds:

Corollary 1.4

Equivalent conditions for \(n, F\) given:

  1. \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(-m))=0\) for \(i\leqslant n\) and \(m\) large.

  2. (i bis)

    \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(\cdot))\) is a finitely generated \(S\)-module for \(i\leqslant n\).

  3. \(\operatorname{prof}(F_x)>n\) for every closed point \(x\) of \(X\).

  4. \(\operatorname{prof}(\widetilde{F}_x)>n+1\) for every closed point \(x\) of \(\widetilde{X}\).

The interest of corollaries 1.3 and 1.4 is that they express a global condition (i) or (i bis) in terms of local conditions, namely the vanishing of local invariants such as \(\operatorname{H}_x^i(X, F_x)\) or \(\operatorname{H}_x^i(X, \widetilde{F}_x)\), or an inequality on the depth. In this form, these results remain trivially valid for an arbitrary projective scheme \(X\), and a very ample invertible sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) on \(X\), as one sees by inducing the latter by means of a suitable projective immersion \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{P}_k^r\). (Of course, conditions 1.3 (ii) and 1.3 (ii bis) are no longer equivalent to the others in this general case, unless one assumes that \(X\) is regular, for example). One may moreover generalize to the case of a projective morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in the following way:

Proposition 1.5

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a projective morphism, with \(S\) noetherian, \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) an invertible Module on \(X\) very ample relative to \(S\), \(F\) a coherent Module on \(X\), flat with respect to \(S\), \(s\) an element of \(S\), \(X_s\) the fibre of \(X\) at \(s\) (considered as a projective scheme over \(k(s)\)), \(F_s\) the sheaf induced on \(X_s\) by \(F\), and finally \(i\) an integer. Suppose that for every closed point \(x\) of \(X_s\), one has \(\operatorname{H}^i_x(F_{s, x})=0\) (for example \(\operatorname{prof}(F_{s, x})>i\)). Then there exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\), such that the same condition is satisfied for the \(s'\in U\). Moreover, for such a \(U\), one has

\[ \operatorname{R}^if_\ast(F(-m))= 0 \; \text{for}\; m \; \text{large, } \]

and if \(\mathscr{S}\) is a quasi-coherent graded algebra on \(S\), generated by \(\mathscr{S}^1\), and which defines \(X\) with \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) as \(X=\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S})\), \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)=\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S}(1))\), then the \(S\)-module

\[ \operatorname{R}^if_\ast(F(\cdot)) = \coprod_{m\in\mathbf{Z}}\operatorname{R}^if_\ast(F(m)) \]

is finitely generated over \(U\).

Let us embed \(X\) in an \(X'=\mathbf{P}_S^r\) in such a way that \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) is induced by \(\mathscr{O}_{P}(1)\) (this is possible provided one replaces \(S\) by an affine neighbourhood of \(s\)). We set3 for every integer \(j\), and every \(t\in S\):

\[ \tag{7} {}\underline{E}^j(t)= \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^j_{\mathscr{O}_{X'_t}} (F_{t'}, \mathscr{O}_{X'_t}(-r-1)) \]

Hence \(\underline{E}^j(t)\) is a coherent Module on \(X_t\); I claim that for \(t\) variable, the family of these Modules is "constructible" in the following sense: for every \(t\in S\) there exists a non-empty open set \(V\) of the closure \(\overline{t}\), endowed with the induced reduced structure, and a coherent Module \(\underline{E}^j(V)\) on \(X_V=X\times_SV\), flat relative to \(V\), such that for every \(t'\in V\), \(\underline{E}^j(t)\) is isomorphic to the Module induced by \(\underline{E}^j(V)\) on \(X_t\). To verify this assertion, setting \(Z=\overline{t}\) endowed with the induced structure, one considers the coherent Modules

\[ \underline{E}^j(Z)= \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^j_{\mathscr{O}_{X'_Z}} (F_Z, \mathscr{O}_{X'_Z}(-r-1)) \]

(where the index \(Z\) again means that one induces over \(Z\)), and one takes for \(V\) a non-empty open set of \(Z\) such that the Modules \(\underline{E}^j(Z)\) are flat over \(V\): this is possible, for one checks at once that \(\underline{E}^j(Z)=0\) for \(j\) not contained in the interval \([0, r]\), and one may apply SGA 1 IV 6.11. One then takes for \(\underline{E}^j(V)=\underline{E}^j(Z)|_{X_V}\), and one verifies easily that it answers the question.

From the preceding remark it follows that there exists a finite partition of \(S\) formed by sets \(V_\alpha\) of the form \(V=V(t)\) as above, (noetherian induction), and applying Serre's theorem EGA III 2.2.1 to the \(\underline{E}^j(V_i)\), one sees that there exists an integer \(m_0\) such that

\[ \operatorname{R}^if_{V_\alpha\ast}(\underline{E}^j(V_\alpha))=0\textup{ pour } i\neq 0, m\geqslant m_0, \textup{ pour }j, \]

whence it follows, using the flatness of \(\underline{E}^j(V_\alpha)\) with respect to \(V_\alpha\) and easy Künneth-type relations (cf. EGA III par. 7), that

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X_t, \underline{E}^j(t)(m))=0\textup{ pour } i\neq 0, m\geqslant m_0, \textup{ pour }j, \]

for every \(t\in V_\alpha\), hence for every \(t\) since the \( V_\alpha\) cover \(S\). From this and from the spectral sequence of the global Ext there follows, thanks to 1.1, as in the proof of 1.2, an isomorphism

\[ \tag{8} {}\operatorname{H}^i(X_t, F_t(-m))' \overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X_t, \underline{E}^{r-i}(t)(m))\textup{ pour } m\geqslant m_0, \]

every integer \(i\), and every \(t\in S\).

Let us now use the hypothesis made on \(F_s\), which is written

\[ \tag{9} {}\underline{E}^{r-i}(s)=0, \]

and which, thanks to (8), is equivalent to

\[ \tag{10} {}\operatorname{H}^i(X_s, F_s(-m))=0 \; \text{for}\; m\geqslant m_0. \]

Since \(F\) and hence \(F(-m)\) is flat with respect to \(S\), it follows, by the Künneth-type relations already invoked, that (for given \(m\)) the same relation (10) holds with \(s\) replaced by a \(t\) near \(s\), in particular for every generization \(t\) of \(s\). By virtue of (8), one will therefore have, for such a generization

\[ \tag{11} {}\underline{E}^{r-i}(t)=0, \]

now the set of \(t\in s\) for which this relation is true is evidently a constructible set (for it induces an open set on each \(V_\alpha\)); since it contains the generizations of \(s\), it contains an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\). This proves the first assertion of 1.5. Moreover, for \(t\in U\), one concludes from (11) and (8) that

\[ \tag{12} {}\operatorname{H}^i(X_t, F_t(-m)=0 \; \text{for}\; m\geqslant m_0, \; t\in U, \]

which, by virtue of the Künneth-type relations, implies (in fact, is much stronger than)

\[ \tag{13} {}\operatorname{R}^if_\ast(F(-m))=0 \; \text{on}\; U, \; \text{for} \; m\geqslant m_0. \]

This proves the second assertion of 1.5. Finally the last one follows at once, by proceeding as at the beginning of the proof of 1.3.

Remark 1.6

The proof simplifies notably (by eliminating all considerations of constructibility) when one already assumes that the hypothesis made for \(s\in S\) is satisfied at all the \(s'\in S\). In fact, when one makes the hypothesis that \(F_s\) has depth \(>i\) at the closed points of \(X\), one has at one's disposal a general statement, of local nature on \(X\), which says that the same condition is satisfied for all the \(X_t\), provided one replaces \(X\) by a suitable open neighbourhood of the fibre \(X_s\) (in other words, a certain part of \(X\), defined by conditions on the Modules induced by \(F\) on the fibres, is open, cf. EGA IV). Since \(f\) is here proper, one may therefore take this neighbourhood of the form \(f^{-1}(U)\), which recovers the first assertion of 1.5 without painful dévissage. In this general case, one can still prove by the method of loc. cit. that the first assertion of 1.5 (proved here by global means, using that \(X\) is projective over \(S\)) again follows from a purely local statement on \(X\) (which the reader will make explicit if he judges it useful).

\[ \tag{2} {}\Omega_{X/k}^r= \mathscr{O}_{\mathbf{P}_k^r}(-r-1). \]

blocks.html:

blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3

Equivalent conditions for \(i, F\) given:

  1. \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(-m))=0\) for \(m\) large.

  2. (i bis)

FAC or EGA III 2.2.1 and 2.3.2).

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (ii) by virtue of corollary 1.2.

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (ii bis) because \(\Omega_{X/k}^r\) is locally isomorphic to \(\mathscr{O}_X\).

(ii bis) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iii) by virtue of the local duality theorem for \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) (which is a regular local ring of dimension \(r\)), according to which the "dual" of \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{r-i}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \mathscr{O}_X)_x\) is identified with \(\operatorname{H}^i_x(F_x)\) (V 2.1).

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{r-i}_{\mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{X}}}(\widetilde{F}, \mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{X}})=0 \]

(thanks to the fact that \(\widetilde{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is faithfully flat, so that the inverse image of \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{r-i}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \mathscr{O}_X)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{r-i}_{\mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{X}}}(\widetilde{F}, \mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{X}})\)) and this last relation is equivalent to (iv) by the local duality theorem for the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\), which is regular of dimension \(r+1\).

In particular, applying this to all the \(i\leqslant n\), one finds:

blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4

Equivalent conditions for \(n, F\) given:

  1. \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(-m))=0\) for \(i\leqslant n\) and \(m\) large.

  2. (i bis)

    \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(\cdot))\) is a finitely generated \(S\)-module for \(i\leqslant n\).

  3. \(\operatorname{prof}(F_x)>n\) for every closed point \(x\) of \(X\).

  4. \(\operatorname{prof}(\widetilde{F}_x)>n+1\) for every closed point \(x\) of \(\widetilde{X}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.5

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a projective morphism, with \(S\) noetherian, \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) an invertible Module on \(X\) very ample relative to \(S\), \(F\) a coherent Module on \(X\), flat with respect to \(S\), \(s\) an element of \(S\), \(X_s\) the fibre of \(X\) at \(s\) (considered as a projective scheme over \(k(s)\)), \(F_s\) the sheaf induced on \(X_s\) by \(F\), and finally \(i\) an integer. Suppose that for every closed point \(x\) of \(X_s\), one has \(\operatorname{H}^i_x(F_{s, x})=0\) (for example \(\operatorname{prof}(F_{s, x})>i\)). Then there exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\), such that the same condition is satisfied for the \(s'\in U\). Moreover, for such a \(U\), one has

\[ \operatorname{R}^if_\ast(F(-m))= 0 \; \text{for}\; m \; \text{large, } \]

and if \(\mathscr{S}\) is a quasi-coherent graded algebra on \(S\), generated by \(\mathscr{S}^1\), and which defines \(X\) with \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) as \(X=\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S})\), \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)=\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S}(1))\), then the \(S\)-module

\[ \operatorname{R}^if_\ast(F(\cdot)) = \coprod_{m\in\mathbf{Z}}\operatorname{R}^if_\ast(F(m)) \]

Let us embed \(X\) in an \(X'=\mathbf{P}_S^r\) in such a way that \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) is induced by \(\mathscr{O}_{P}(1)\) (this is possible provided one replaces \(S\) by an affine neighbourhood of \(s\)). We set3 for every integer \(j\), and every \(t\in S\):
\[ \tag{7} {}\underline{E}^j(t)= \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^j_{\mathscr{O}_{X'_t}} (F_{t'}, \mathscr{O}_{X'_t}(-r-1)) \]

Hence \(\underline{E}^j(t)\) is a coherent Module on \(X_t\); I claim that for \(t\) variable, the family of these Modules is "constructible" in the following sense: for every \(t\in S\) there exists a non-empty open set \(V\) of the closure \(\overline{t}\), endowed with the induced reduced structure, and a coherent Module \(\underline{E}^j(V)\) on \(X_V=X\times_SV\), flat relative to \(V\), such that for every \(t'\in V\), \(\underline{E}^j(t)\) is isomorphic to the Module induced by \(\underline{E}^j(V)\) on \(X_t\). To verify this assertion, setting \(Z=\overline{t}\) endowed with the induced structure, one considers the coherent Modules
\[ \underline{E}^j(Z)= \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^j_{\mathscr{O}_{X'_Z}} (F_Z, \mathscr{O}_{X'_Z}(-r-1)) \]

(where the index \(Z\) again means that one induces over \(Z\)), and one takes for \(V\) a non-empty open set of \(Z\) such that the Modules \(\underline{E}^j(Z)\) are flat over \(V\): this is possible, for one checks at once that \(\underline{E}^j(Z)=0\) for \(j\) not contained in the interval \([0, r]\), and one may apply SGA 1 IV 6.11. One then takes for \(\underline{E}^j(V)=\underline{E}^j(Z)|_{X_V}\), and one verifies easily that it answers the question.

From the preceding remark it follows that there exists a finite partition of \(S\) formed by sets \(V_\alpha\) of the form \(V=V(t)\) as above, (noetherian induction), and applying Serre's theorem EGA III 2.2.1 to the \(\underline{E}^j(V_i)\), one sees that there exists an integer \(m_0\) such that
\[ \operatorname{R}^if_{V_\alpha\ast}(\underline{E}^j(V_\alpha))=0\textup{ pour } i\neq 0, m\geqslant m_0, \textup{ pour }j, \]
whence it follows, using the flatness of \(\underline{E}^j(V_\alpha)\) with respect to \(V_\alpha\) and easy Künneth-type relations (cf. EGA III par. 7), that
\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X_t, \underline{E}^j(t)(m))=0\textup{ pour } i\neq 0, m\geqslant m_0, \textup{ pour }j, \]

for every \(t\in V_\alpha\), hence for every \(t\) since the \( V_\alpha\) cover \(S\). From this and from the spectral sequence of the global Ext there follows, thanks to 1.1, as in the proof of 1.2, an isomorphism
\[ \tag{8} {}\operatorname{H}^i(X_t, F_t(-m))' \overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X_t, \underline{E}^{r-i}(t)(m))\textup{ pour } m\geqslant m_0, \]
every integer \(i\), and every \(t\in S\).
Let us now use the hypothesis made on \(F_s\), which is written
\[ \tag{9} {}\underline{E}^{r-i}(s)=0, \]
and which, thanks to (8), is equivalent to
\[ \tag{10} {}\operatorname{H}^i(X_s, F_s(-m))=0 \; \text{for}\; m\geqslant m_0. \]

Since \(F\) and hence \(F(-m)\) is flat with respect to \(S\), it follows, by the Künneth-type relations already invoked, that (for given \(m\)) the same relation (10) holds with \(s\) replaced by a \(t\) near \(s\), in particular for every generization \(t\) of \(s\). By virtue of (8), one will therefore have, for such a generization
\[ \tag{11} {}\underline{E}^{r-i}(t)=0, \]

now the set of \(t\in s\) for which this relation is true is evidently a constructible set (for it induces an open set on each \(V_\alpha\)); since it contains the generizations of \(s\), it contains an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\). This proves the first assertion of 1.5. Moreover, for \(t\in U\), one concludes from (11) and (8) that
\[ \tag{12} {}\operatorname{H}^i(X_t, F_t(-m)=0 \; \text{for}\; m\geqslant m_0, \; t\in U, \]
which, by virtue of the Künneth-type relations, implies (in fact, is much stronger than)
\[ \tag{13} {}\operatorname{R}^if_\ast(F(-m))=0 \; \text{on}\; U, \; \text{for} \; m\geqslant m_0. \]
This proves the second assertion of 1.5. Finally the last one follows at once, by proceeding as at the beginning of the proof of 1.3.

blocks.title: Remark 1.6
blocks.html:
Remark 1.6

The proof simplifies notably (by eliminating all considerations of constructibility) when one already assumes that the hypothesis made for \(s\in S\) is satisfied at all the \(s'\in S\). In fact, when one makes the hypothesis that \(F_s\) has depth \(>i\) at the closed points of \(X\), one has at one's disposal a general statement, of local nature on \(X\), which says that the same condition is satisfied for all the \(X_t\), provided one replaces \(X\) by a suitable open neighbourhood of the fibre \(X_s\) (in other words, a certain part of \(X\), defined by conditions on the Modules induced by \(F\) on the fibres, is open, cf. EGA IV). Since \(f\) is here proper, one may therefore take this neighbourhood of the form \(f^{-1}(U)\), which recovers the first assertion of 1.5 without painful dévissage. In this general case, one can still prove by the method of loc. cit. that the first assertion of 1.5 (proved here by global means, using that \(X\) is projective over \(S\)) again follows from a purely local statement on \(X\) (which the reader will make explicit if he judges it useful).

footnotes.html: J.-P. Serre, Faisceaux algébriques Cohérents, Ann. of Math. t.61 (1955) 197-278.
For a more general duality theorem, cf. the Séminaire Hartshorne cited at the end of Exp. IV.
The first part of 1.5 can be obtained at once by applying the purely local statement EGA IV 12.3.4 to the \(\underline{E}^j\) above, which short-circuits the greater part of the proof that follows.

pages.title: Lefschetz theory for a projective morphism: Grauert's comparison theorem
pages.html:
2 Lefschetz theory for a projective morphism: Grauert's comparison theorem

This is the following theorem:

Theorem 2.1

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a projective morphism, with \(S\) noetherian, \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) an invertible Module on \(X\), ample relative to \(S\), \(Y\) the prescheme of zeros of a section \(t\) of \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\), \(J\) the ideal defining \(Y\), \(X_n\) the subprescheme of \(X\) defined by \(J^{n+1}\), \(\widehat{X}\) the formal completion of \(X\) along \(Y\), \(\widehat{f}:\widehat{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) the composed morphism \(\widehat{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), \(F\) a coherent Module on \(X\), flat relative to \(S\). Suppose moreover that for every \(s\in S\), the Module \(F_s\) induced on the fibre \(X_s\) is of depth \(>n\) at the points of the said fibre, and that \(t\) is \(F\)-regular. Under these conditions:

  1. The canonical homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{R}^if_\ast(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{R}^i\widehat{f}_\ast(\widehat{F}) \]

    is an isomorphism for \(i<n\), a monomorphism for \(i=n\).

  2. The canonical homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{R}^i\widehat{f}_\ast(\widehat{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_m \operatorname{R}^if_\ast(F_m) \]

    is an isomorphism for \(i\leqslant n\).

Proof▾

One reduces at once to the case where \(S\) is affine, and then to proving the

Corollary 2.2

Under the conditions of 2.1 suppose moreover \(S\) affine. Then:

  1. The canonical homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{H}^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(\widehat{X}, \widehat{F}) \]

    is an isomorphism for \(i<n\), a monomorphism for \(i=n\).

  2. The canonical homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{H}^i(\widehat{X}, \widehat{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_m \operatorname{H}^i(X_m, F_m) \]

    is an isomorphism for \(i\leqslant n\).

Replacing \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) by a tensor power, and \(t\) by a power of \(t\), we may assume \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) very ample relative to \(S\). On the other hand, \(t\), hence \(t^m\), being \(F\)-regular, multiplication by \(t^m\), viewed as a homomorphism from \(F(-m)\) into \(F\), is injective, hence for every \(m\geqslant 0\) we have an exact sequence:

\[ \tag{14} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(-m) \xrightarrow{t^m} F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}0, \]

whence an exact cohomology sequence

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X, F(-m)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(X, F_m) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{i+1}(X, F(-m)). \]

Now by virtue of 1.5 we have \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(-m))=0\) for \(i\leqslant n\), and \(m\) large enough, which proves the

Lemma 2.3

For \(m\) large, the canonical homomorphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(X, F_m) \]

is bijective if \(i<n\), injective if \(i=n\).

-2 This shows that for \(i<n\), the projective system \((\operatorname{H}^i(X_m, F_m))_{m\geqslant 0}\) is essentially constant, a fortiori satisfies the Mittag-Leffler condition, hence (taking into account that \(\widehat{F}= \varprojlim F_m\)) we conclude (ii) by \(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 13.3. On the other hand (i) follows trivially, taking into account 2.3.

Corollary 2.4

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a projective and flat morphism, with \(S\) locally noetherian, \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) an invertible Module on \(X\), ample relative to \(S\), \(t\) a section of this Module which is \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-regular, \(Y\) the subprescheme of zeros of \(t\), \(\widehat{X}\) the formal completion of \(X\) along \(Y\). Suppose that for every \(s\in S\), \(X_s\) is of depth \(\geqslant 1\) (resp. of depth \(\geqslant 2\)) at its closed points. Then for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\), the functor

\[ F \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \widehat{F} \]

from the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(U\), into the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(\widehat{X}\), is faithful (resp. fully faithful, i.e. the Lefschetz condition (\(\operatorname{Lef}\)) of X 2 is satisfied).

Let us introduce, for two locally free Modules \(F\) and \(G\) on \(U\), the Module

\[ H=\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_U}(F, G) \]

we are reduced to proving that the canonical homomorphism

\[ \tag{15} {}\operatorname{H}^0(U, H) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(\widehat{U}, \widehat{H}) \]

is injective (resp. bijective). Now the Modules \(H_t\) are of depth \(\geqslant 1\) (resp. \(\geqslant 2\)) at the closed points of \(X_t\), so we can apply 2.1, which implies the conclusion 2.4 in the case where \(U=X\). In the case of an arbitrary \(U\), we note that the question is local on \(S\), hence we may assume \(S\) affine. Then every coherent Module on \(X\) is a quotient of a locally free coherent Module, (since \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) is an absolutely ample invertible Module on \(X\)). Since the dual Module \(H'=\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(H, \mathscr{O}_U)\) extends to a coherent Module on \(X\), which is therefore isomorphic to a cokernel of a homomorphism of locally free Modules on \(X\), it follows by transposition that one can find a homomorphism

\[ u':{L'}^0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {L'}^1 \]

of locally free Modules on \(X\), inducing a homomorphism

\[ u:L^0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L^1 \]

of locally free Modules on \(U\), such that one has an exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L^0 \xrightarrow{u} L^1. \]

Using the five lemma (which becomes the three lemma), and the left exactness of the functor \(H^0\), we are reduced to proving that (15) is injective (resp. bijective) when one replaces \(H\) in it by \(L^0\), \(L^1\), which reduces us to the case where \(H\) is induced by a locally free Module \(H'\) on \(X\). Moreover in the non-resp. case this reduction is even unnecessary, for the kernel of (15) is in any case formed of the sections of \(H\) on \(U\) which vanish in a suitable open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(Y\); now the restriction homomorphism \(\operatorname{H}^0(U, H)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^0(V, H)\) is injective, since \(H\) is of depth \(\geqslant 1\) at the points of every closed part \(Z\) of \(X\) not meeting \(Y\) (cf. lemma below). In the resp. case, we are reduced to proving that

\[ H^0(X, H')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^0(U, H') \]

is bijective, which results from the fact that \(H'\) is of depth \(\geqslant 2\) at all the points of a closed set \(Z=X- U\) of \(X\) not meeting \(Y\). It thus remains simply to prove the

Lemma 2.5

Let \(F\) be a coherent Module on \(X\), flat relative to \(S\), such that for every \(s\in S\), \(F_s\) is of depth \(\geqslant n\) at every closed point of \(X_s\). Then for every closed part \(Z\) of \(X\) not meeting \(Y\), \(F\) is of depth \(\geqslant n\) at all the points of \(Z\).

Indeed, for every \(x\in X\), setting \(s=f(x)\), we have

\[ \tag{16} {}\operatorname{prof}(F_x)\geqslant \operatorname{prof}(F_{s, x}), \]

as one sees by lifting in any way whatsoever a maximal \(F_{s, x}\)-regular sequence of elements of \(\mathfrak{r}(\mathscr{O}_{X_s, x})\), which gives an \(F_x\)-regular sequence by virtue of SGA 1 IV 5.7. Now if \(x\) belongs to a \(Z\) as in lemma 2.5, then \(x\) is necessarily closed in \(X_s\), in other words, \(Z\) is finite over \(S\). Indeed \(Z\) (endowed with a structure induced by \(X\)) is projective over \(S\) as a closed subprescheme of \(X\) which is so, and \(Z\) is affine over \(S\) as a closed subprescheme of \(X- Y\), which is so.

Q.E.D.
Remark 2.6

Suppose that for every \(s\in S\), the section \(t_s\) of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_s}(1)\) induced by \(t\) is \(\mathscr{O}_{X_s}\)-regular (which implies by SGA 1 IV 5.7 that \(t\) is \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-regular). Then the hypotheses made are stable under base extension \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) (\(S'\) locally noetherian). Hence the conclusion remains valid after any base change.

Proof▾

Corollary 2.2

    is an isomorphism for \(i\leqslant n\).

\[ \tag{14} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(-m) \xrightarrow{t^m} F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}0, \]

whence an exact cohomology sequence

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X, F(-m)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(X, F_m) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{i+1}(X, F(-m)). \]

Now by virtue of 1.5 we have \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F(-m))=0\) for \(i\leqslant n\), and \(m\) large enough, which proves the

Lemma 2.3

For \(m\) large, the canonical homomorphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(X, F_m) \]

is bijective if \(i<n\), injective if \(i=n\).

-2 This shows that for \(i<n\), the projective system \((\operatorname{H}^i(X_m, F_m))_{m\geqslant 0}\) is essentially constant, a fortiori satisfies the Mittag-Leffler condition, hence (taking into account that \(\widehat{F}= \varprojlim F_m\)) we conclude (ii) by \(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 13.3. On the other hand (i) follows trivially, taking into account 2.3.

Corollary 2.4

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a projective and flat morphism, with \(S\) locally noetherian, \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) an invertible Module on \(X\), ample relative to \(S\), \(t\) a section of this Module which is \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-regular, \(Y\) the subprescheme of zeros of \(t\), \(\widehat{X}\) the formal completion of \(X\) along \(Y\). Suppose that for every \(s\in S\), \(X_s\) is of depth \(\geqslant 1\) (resp. of depth \(\geqslant 2\)) at its closed points. Then for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\), the functor

\[ F \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \widehat{F} \]

from the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(U\), into the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(\widehat{X}\), is faithful (resp. fully faithful, i.e. the Lefschetz condition (\(\operatorname{Lef}\)) of X 2 is satisfied).

Let us introduce, for two locally free Modules \(F\) and \(G\) on \(U\), the Module

\[ H=\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_U}(F, G) \]

we are reduced to proving that the canonical homomorphism

\[ \tag{15} {}\operatorname{H}^0(U, H) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(\widehat{U}, \widehat{H}) \]

is injective (resp. bijective). Now the Modules \(H_t\) are of depth \(\geqslant 1\) (resp. \(\geqslant 2\)) at the closed points of \(X_t\), so we can apply 2.1, which implies the conclusion 2.4 in the case where \(U=X\). In the case of an arbitrary \(U\), we note that the question is local on \(S\), hence we may assume \(S\) affine. Then every coherent Module on \(X\) is a quotient of a locally free coherent Module, (since \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) is an absolutely ample invertible Module on \(X\)). Since the dual Module \(H'=\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(H, \mathscr{O}_U)\) extends to a coherent Module on \(X\), which is therefore isomorphic to a cokernel of a homomorphism of locally free Modules on \(X\), it follows by transposition that one can find a homomorphism

\[ u':{L'}^0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {L'}^1 \]

of locally free Modules on \(X\), inducing a homomorphism

\[ u:L^0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L^1 \]

of locally free Modules on \(U\), such that one has an exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L^0 \xrightarrow{u} L^1. \]

Using the five lemma (which becomes the three lemma), and the left exactness of the functor \(H^0\), we are reduced to proving that (15) is injective (resp. bijective) when one replaces \(H\) in it by \(L^0\), \(L^1\), which reduces us to the case where \(H\) is induced by a locally free Module \(H'\) on \(X\). In the resp. case, we are reduced to proving that

\[ H^0(X, H')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^0(U, H') \]

is bijective, which results from the fact that \(H'\) is of depth \(\geqslant 2\) at all the points of a closed set \(Z=X- U\) of \(X\) not meeting \(Y\). It thus remains simply to prove the

Lemma 2.5

Let \(F\) be a coherent Module on \(X\), flat relative to \(S\), such that for every \(s\in S\), \(F_s\) is of depth \(\geqslant n\) at every closed point of \(X_s\). Then for every closed part \(Z\) of \(X\) not meeting \(Y\), \(F\) is of depth \(\geqslant n\) at all the points of \(Z\).

Indeed, for every \(x\in X\), setting \(s=f(x)\), we have

\[ \tag{16} {}\operatorname{prof}(F_x)\geqslant \operatorname{prof}(F_{s, x}), \]

as one sees by lifting in any way whatsoever a maximal \(F_{s, x}\)-regular sequence of elements of \(\mathfrak{r}(\mathscr{O}_{X_s, x})\), which gives an \(F_x\)-regular sequence by virtue of SGA 1 IV 5.7. Now if \(x\) belongs to a \(Z\) as in lemma 2.5, then \(x\) is necessarily closed in \(X_s\), in other words, \(Z\) is finite over \(S\). blocks.html:
Remark 2.6

Suppose that for every \(s\in S\), the section \(t_s\) of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_s}(1)\) induced by \(t\) is \(\mathscr{O}_{X_s}\)-regular (which implies by SGA 1 IV 5.7 that \(t\) is \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-regular). Then the hypotheses made are stable under base extension \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) (\(S'\) locally noetherian). Hence the conclusion remains valid after any base change.

pages.title: Lefschetz theory for a projective morphism: existence theorem
pages.html:
3 Lefschetz theory for a projective morphism: existence theorem

Theorem 3.1

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a projective morphism, with \(S\) noetherian, \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) an invertible Module on \(X\) ample relative to \(S\), \(X_0\) the subprescheme of zeros of a section \(t\) of \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\), \(\widehat{X}\) the formal completion of \(X\) along \(X_0\), \(\mathfrak{F}\) a coherent Module on \(\widehat{X}\), \(F_0\) the Module it induces on \(X_0\). We assume moreover:

  1. \(\mathfrak{F}\) is flat relative to \(S\).

  2. For every \(s\in S\), the section \(t_s\) induced by \(t\) on \(X_s\) is \(\mathfrak{F}_s\)-regular (which implies that \(F_0\) is likewise flat relative to \(S\), cf. SGA 1 IV 5.7).

  3. For every \(s\in S\), \(F_{0s}\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at the closed points of \(X_{0s}\).

We assume moreover that \(S\) admits an ample invertible sheaf. Under these conditions, there exists a coherent Module \(F\) on \(X\), and an isomorphism of its formal completion \(\widehat{F}\) with \(\mathfrak{F}\).

This statement will follow from the next one:

Corollary 3.2

Under conditions a), b), c) above we have the following:

  1. The Module \(\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F})\) on \(S\) is coherent, hence for every \(n\), the Module \(\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}(n))\) on \(S\) is coherent.

  2. For \(n\) large, the canonical homomorphism \(\widehat{f}^\ast\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}(n))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathfrak{F}(n)\) is surjective.

Let us grant the corollary for the moment, and prove 3.1. Thanks to the last hypothesis made in 3.1, we may reduce to the case where \(X=\mathbf{P}^r_S\), replacing \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\), \(t\) by a suitable power if necessary. I claim that we may further assume that for every \(s\), we have \(t_s\neq 0\). Otherwise, indeed, we have \(\mathfrak{F}_s=0\) by b), or equivalently by Nakayama, \(F_{0s}=0\) i.e. \(s\) does not belong to the image of \(\operatorname{Supp} F_0\) under the morphism \(f_0:X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) induced by \(f\). Now this image \(S'\) is open by virtue of a), b) since \(F_0\) is flat relative to \(S\), and it is clear that it suffices to prove the conclusion of 3.1 in the situation obtained by restricting over \(S'\), for the coherent Module \(F'\) on \(X|_{S'}\) thus obtained will be the restriction of a coherent Module \(F\) on \(X\), which will answer the question. We may therefore assume that, in addition to hypotheses a), b), c), the following hypotheses are also satisfied:

  1. a\('\))

    \(\mathscr{O}_X\) is flat relative to \(S\).

  2. b\('\))

    For every \(s\in S\), the section \(t_s\) is \(\mathscr{O}_{X_s}\)-regular.

  3. c\('\))

    For every \(s\in S\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{0s}}\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at the closed points of \(X_{0s}\). (It suffices to choose \(X=\mathbf{P}_S^r\) with \(r\geqslant 3\), which is permissible).

Now 3.2 implies that one can find an epimorphism

\[ \tag{17} \widehat{L} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathfrak{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

where \(L\) is a Module on \(X\) of the form \(f_\ast(G)(-n)\), \(G\) being a locally free coherent Module on \(S\): indeed it suffices, for \(n\) large, to represent the coherent Module \(\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F})\) on \(S\) as a quotient of such a \(G\). On the other hand, hypotheses a), b), c) on \(f\), \(t\) imply that \(\widehat{L}\) satisfies the same conditions a), b), c) as \(\mathfrak{F}\). One concludes easily that the same holds for the kernel of (17), to which one may therefore apply the same argument, so that \(\mathfrak{F}\) is represented as the cokernel of a homomorphism

\[ \tag{18} \widehat{L'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widehat{L}, \]

where \(L\), \(L'\) are locally free Modules on \(X\). Now by virtue of a\('\)) and the second part of c\('\)), and of 2.1 or 2.4 as one chooses, the homomorphism (18) comes from a homomorphism \(L'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L\) of Modules on \(X\). It now suffices to take for \(F\) the cokernel of \(L'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L\), and we are done.

It remains to prove 3.2. This had been done in the seminar by a somewhat tedious expedient, consisting in interpreting everything in terms of cohomology on the punctured projecting cone of \(X\) relative to \(S\), in order to be able to reduce to theorem 2.1. A more direct and more satisfactory way (although substantially identical) now seems to me the following. It consists in noting that in IX, nº 2 (and with the notations of that exposé) the hypothesis that the morphism \(f:\mathscr{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{X}'\) be adic intervenes nowhere in the proof of 2.1, via \(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 13.7.7; it suffices to assume instead that \(\mathscr{X}\) is likewise adic, and to choose two ideals of definition \(\mathscr{J}\) for \(\mathscr{X}'\), \(\mathscr{I}\) for \(\mathscr{X}\), such that \(\mathscr{J}\mathscr{O}_\mathscr{X}\subset\mathscr{I}\), and to define \(\mathscr{S}=\operatorname{gr}_\mathscr{J}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'})\), and consider \(\operatorname{gr}_\mathscr{I}(\mathfrak{F})\). In any case, 2.1 can be applied directly to the morphism \(\widehat{f}:\widehat{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) considered in the present section, where one simply takes \(\mathscr{J}=0\). Thus to verify that \(\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F})\) is coherent, it suffices by virtue of loc. cit. to verify that \(\operatorname{R}^if_{0\ast}(\operatorname{gr}_\mathscr{I}(\mathfrak{F}))\) is coherent on \(S\) for \(i=0, 1\); for this one notes that by virtue of a) and b), the Module under consideration is none other than \(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\operatorname{R}^if_{0\ast}(F_0(-m))\), which is indeed coherent by virtue of hypothesis c) and of 1.5.

This proves 3.2 (i). For 3.2 (ii), we shall need the

Lemma 3.3

Under conditions a), b), c) of 3.1, set

\[ G_m= \widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}(\cdot)_m) = \coprod_n\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}_m(n)) \]

Then the projective system \((G_m)\) satisfies the Mittag-Leffler condition.

We may assume \(S\) affine, with ring \(A\). Let then \(\mathscr{S}\) be a finitely generated graded \(A\)-algebra with positive degrees, and \(t'\in\mathscr{S}_1\), such that \(X\) embeds in \(\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S})\), \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) being induced by \(\mathbf{Proj}(\mathscr{S}(1))\) and the section \(t\) being the image of \(t'\). Endow \(\mathscr{S}\) with the \(\mathscr{J}\)-adic filtration, where \(\mathscr{J}=t'\mathscr{S}\), and consider the projective system of the \(\mathfrak{F}(\cdot)_m\) in the category of abelian sheaves on \(X_0\). We are again under the preliminary conditions of \(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 13.7.71 and moreover \(\operatorname{H}^i(X_0, \operatorname{gr}(\mathfrak{F}(\cdot))\) is a finitely generated Module over \(\operatorname{gr}_\mathscr{J}(\mathscr{S})\) for \(i=0, 1\). Indeed, since \(t'\) is \(\mathfrak{F}\)-regular, one immediately sees that as a Module over \((\mathscr{S} /t'\mathscr{S})[T]\) (of which \(\operatorname{gr}_\mathscr{J}(\mathscr{S})\) is a quotient), the Module under consideration is identified with \(\operatorname{H}^i(X_0, F_0(\cdot))\otimes_{\mathscr{S} /t'\mathscr{S}}(\mathscr{S} /t'\mathscr{S})[T]\), and by virtue of 1.5, \(\operatorname{H}^i(X_0, F_0(\cdot))\) is finitely generated over \(\mathscr{S}\), hence over \(\mathscr{S} /t'\mathscr{S}\), for \(i=0, 1\), which proves our assertion. Consequently we are under the conditions of application of \(0_{\text{III}}\) 13.7.7 with \(n=1\), which proves 3.3.

This point being acquired (and still assuming \(S\) affine, which is permissible for proving 3.2 (ii)) let \(m_0\) be such that \(m\geqslant m_0\) implies \(\operatorname{Im}(G_m\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0)= \operatorname{Im}(G_{m_0}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0)\), so that the two members are also equal to \(\operatorname{Im}(\varprojlim G_m\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0) = \operatorname{Im}(\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}(\cdot))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_\ast\mathfrak{F}_0(\cdot))\). Let us now remark that for \(n\) large, \(\mathfrak{F}_{m_0}(n)\) is generated by its sections, hence \(\mathfrak{F}_0(n)\) is generated by sections that lift to \(\mathfrak{F}_{m_0}\), hence (thanks to the choice of \(m_0\)) that lift to \(\mathfrak{F}\). Hence the sections of \(\mathfrak{F}\) generate \(\mathfrak{F}_0\), hence also \(\mathfrak{F}\) thanks to Nakayama. This proves 3.2 (ii), hence 3.1.

Corollary 3.4

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a projective and flat morphism, with \(S\) locally noetherian, \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) an invertible Module on \(X\), ample relative to \(S\), \(t\) a section of this Module such that for every \(s\in S\), the section \(t_s\) induced on the fibre \(X_s\) is \(\mathscr{O}_{X_s}\)-regular, \(X_0\) the subprescheme of zeros of \(t\), \(\widehat{X}\) the formal completion of \(X\) along \(X_0\). We assume that for every \(s\in S\), \(X_{0s}\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at its closed points, (i.e. \(X_s\) has depth \(\geqslant 3\) at the closed points of \(X_{0s}\)) and \(X_s\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at its closed points. Under these conditions, the pair \((X, X_0)\) satisfies the effective Lefschetz condition (\(\operatorname{Leff}\)) of X 2, i.e.:

  1. For every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(X_0\) in \(X\), the functor

    \[ F \rightsquigarrow \widehat{F} \]

    from the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(U\) to the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(\widehat{X}\) is fully faithful.

  2. For every locally free coherent Module \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(\widehat{X}\), there exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(X_0\), and a locally free coherent Module \(F\) on \(U\) such that \(\mathfrak{F}\) is isomorphic to \(\widehat{F}\).

Indeed, a) has already been noted in 2.4 under weaker conditions. For b), one applies 3.1 which gives the conclusion, at least if \(S\) is noetherian and admits an absolutely ample invertible Module, in particular if \(S\) is affine. Indeed, if \(F\) is a coherent module on \(X\) such that \(\widehat{F}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathfrak{F}\) hence locally free, it follows that \(F\) is locally free on a neighbourhood \(U\) of \(X_0\), and \(F|U\) will satisfy the desired condition. But let us now note that by virtue of 2.5, for such an \(F\), its image under the immersion \(U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is coherent, and moreover independent of the chosen solution \((U, F)\) (given that two solutions coincide in a neighbourhood of \(X_0\), by virtue of a)). More precisely, one can find a coherent Module \(F\) on \(X\) and an isomorphism \(\widehat{F}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathfrak{F}\), such that \(F\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at all the points of \(X\) that are closed in their fibre, and this determines \(F\) up to a unique isomorphism. Thanks to this uniqueness property, the solutions of the problem found by inducing over the affine open sets of \(S\) glue together, whence an \(F\) coherent on all of \(X\) and an isomorphism \(\widehat{F}\simeq \mathfrak{F}\). Restricting \(F\) to the open set \(U\) of the points at which it is free, one finds what was sought.

Thanks to 2.4 and 3.4, one can exploit, in the situation of a projective algebraic scheme and of a "hyperplane section" thereof, the general facts established in exposés X and XI concerning the conditions (Lef) and (Leff). Thus:

Corollary 3.5

Let \(X\) be a projective algebraic scheme equipped with an ample invertible Module \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\), let \(t\) be a section of this Module which is \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-regular, and let \(X_0\) be the subscheme of zeros of \(t\). Suppose that \(X\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at its closed points (resp. and depth \(\geqslant 3\) at the closed points of \(X_0\)). Then \(\pi_0(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0(X)\) is bijective, in particular \(X\) is connected if and only if \(X_0\) is, and choosing a geometric base point in \(X_0\), \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X)\) is surjective, and more generally for every open set \(U\supset X_0\), the homomorphism \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(U)\) is surjective (resp. the homomorphism \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_U\pi_1(U)\) is bijective). In the resp. case, if one assumes moreover that the local ring of every closed point of \(X\) not in \(X_0\) is pure (3.2) (for example is regular, or merely a complete intersection) then \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X)\) is an isomorphism.

One applies 2.4 and 3.4. One will note that in the resp. case, the hypothesis that \(X\) has depth \(\geqslant 3\) at the closed points of \(X_0\) implies that all the irreducible components of dimension \(\neq 0\) of \(X\) have dimension \(\geqslant 3\) (as one sees by noting that such a component necessarily meets \(X_0\), and by looking at a closed point of the intersection).

Remark

When \(X\) is normal, of dimension \(\geqslant 2\) at all its points, it has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at its closed points and we are under the non-resp. conditions of 3.5. In this case, one has a more elementary proof of the surjectivity of \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X)\) using Bertini's theorem (cf. SGA 1 X.2.10). When one assumes moreover \(X_0\) normal, and \(X\) of dimension \(\geqslant 3\) at all its points, then we are under the resp. conditions of 3.5. In this case, 3.5 was established by Grauert (it turns out indeed that, thanks to the normality hypothesis, one then manages to dispense with the existence theorem 3.1 by expedients). It is this proof of Grauert that was the starting point of the "Lefschetz theory" which is the subject of the present seminar.

Corollary 3.6

Let \(X\), \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\), \(t\), \(X_0\) be as in 3.5. Suppose that \(X\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at its closed points, and that

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X_0, \mathscr{O}_{X_0}(-n))=0 \]

for \(n>0\) and for \(i=1\) (resp. for \(i=1\) and for \(i=2\)), which implies by virtue of 1.4 that \(X_0\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) (resp. \(\geqslant 3\)) at its closed points, i.e. that \(X\) has depth \(\geqslant 3\) (resp. \(\geqslant 4\)) at the closed points of \(X_0\). Under these conditions, for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(X_0\), \(\operatorname{Pic}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(X_0)\) is injective, in particular \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(X_0)\) is injective, (resp. \(\varprojlim_U \operatorname{Pic}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(X_0)\) is bijective). In the resp. case, if one assumes moreover that the local ring of \(X\) at every closed point not in \(X_0\) is parafactorial (3.1) (for example is regular, or more generally a complete intersection), then \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(X_0)\) is bijective.

One applies XI 3.12 and 3.13, noting that the resp. hypothesis implies that the irreducible components of dimension \(\neq 0\) of \(X\) have dimension \(\geqslant 4\). One finds in particular, applying this to the case where \(X\) is a global complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant 4\) in projective space:

Corollary 3.7

Let \(X\) be an algebraic scheme of dimension \(\geqslant 3\), which is a complete intersection in a scheme \(\mathbf{P}_k^r\). Then \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) is the free group generated by the class of the sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\).

One argues by induction on the number of hypersurfaces of which \(X\) is the intersection, applying 3.6 and noting that for a complete intersection \(X\) of dimension \(\geqslant 3\), one has \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, \mathscr{O}_X(n))=0\) for \(i=1, 2\), and all \(n\).

Remark 3.8

In the case where \(X\) is a nonsingular hypersurface, 3.7 is due to Andreotti. The result 3.7 can also be expressed (when \(X\) is nonsingular) by saying that the homogeneous coordinate ring of \(X\) is factorial, and in this form is contained in XI 3.13 (ii). Let us also point out that Serre had given a proof of 3.7 in the nonsingular case, by transcendental means, using a specialization argument to reduce to the case of characteristic 0, where one has Lefschetz's theorem in its classical form. Of course, the fact that the purely algebraic proof given here makes it possible to get rid of nonsingularity hypotheses in the statement of Lefschetz's theorem invites one to reconsider the latter also in the classical case. Cf. the following exposé, which proposes conjectures in this direction.

In corollaries 3.5 and 3.7 we have placed ourselves over a base field, whereas the key theorems 2.4 and 3.4 are valid over an arbitrary base. To generalize these corollaries 3.5 and 3.6 to a general \(S\), we must give workable criteria for a point of \(X\) (flat over \(S\)) to have a "pure" resp. parafactorial local ring. This will be the subject of the next no.

blocks.html: 3 Lefschetz theory for a projective morphism: existence theorem

blocks.title: Theorem 3.1
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.1

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a projective morphism, with \(S\) noetherian, \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) an invertible Module on \(X\) ample relative to \(S\), \(X_0\) the subprescheme of zeros of a section \(t\) of \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\), \(\widehat{X}\) the formal completion of \(X\) along \(X_0\), \(\mathfrak{F}\) a coherent Module on \(\widehat{X}\), \(F_0\) the Module it induces on \(X_0\). We assume moreover:

  1. \(\mathfrak{F}\) is flat relative to \(S\).

  2. For every \(s\in S\), the section \(t_s\) induced by \(t\) on \(X_s\) is \(\mathfrak{F}_s\)-regular (which implies that \(F_0\) is likewise flat relative to \(S\), cf. SGA 1 IV 5.7).

  3. For every \(s\in S\), \(F_{0s}\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at the closed points of \(X_{0s}\).

We assume moreover that \(S\) admits an ample invertible sheaf. Under these conditions, there exists a coherent Module \(F\) on \(X\), and an isomorphism of its formal completion \(\widehat{F}\) with \(\mathfrak{F}\).
This statement will follow from the next one:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2

Under conditions a), b), c) above we have the following:

  1. The Module \(\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F})\) on \(S\) is coherent, hence for every \(n\), the Module \(\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}(n))\) on \(S\) is coherent.

  2. For \(n\) large, the canonical homomorphism \(\widehat{f}^\ast\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}(n))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathfrak{F}(n)\) is surjective.

Let us grant the corollary for the moment, and prove 3.1. Thanks to the last hypothesis made in 3.1, we may reduce to the case where \(X=\mathbf{P}^r_S\), replacing \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\), \(t\) by a suitable power if necessary. I claim that we may further assume that for every \(s\), we have \(t_s\neq 0\). Otherwise, indeed, we have \(\mathfrak{F}_s=0\) by b), or equivalently by Nakayama, \(F_{0s}=0\) i.e. \(s\) does not belong to the image of \(\operatorname{Supp} F_0\) under the morphism \(f_0:X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) induced by \(f\). Now this image \(S'\) is open by virtue of a), b) since \(F_0\) is flat relative to \(S\), and it is clear that it suffices to prove the conclusion of 3.1 in the situation obtained by restricting over \(S'\), for the coherent Module \(F'\) on \(X|_{S'}\) thus obtained will be the restriction of a coherent Module \(F\) on \(X\), which will answer the question. We may therefore assume that, in addition to hypotheses a), b), c), the following hypotheses are also satisfied:

1. a\('\))

    \(\mathscr{O}_X\) is flat relative to \(S\).

  2. b\('\))

    For every \(s\in S\), the section \(t_s\) is \(\mathscr{O}_{X_s}\)-regular.

  3. c\('\))

    For every \(s\in S\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{0s}}\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at the closed points of \(X_{0s}\). (It suffices to choose \(X=\mathbf{P}_S^r\) with \(r\geqslant 3\), which is permissible).
Now 3.2 implies that one can find an epimorphism
\[ \tag{17} \widehat{L} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathfrak{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

where \(L\) is a Module on \(X\) of the form \(f_\ast(G)(-n)\), \(G\) being a locally free coherent Module on \(S\): indeed it suffices, for \(n\) large, to represent the coherent Module \(\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F})\) on \(S\) as a quotient of such a \(G\). On the other hand, hypotheses a), b), c) on \(f\), \(t\) imply that \(\widehat{L}\) satisfies the same conditions a), b), c) as \(\mathfrak{F}\). One concludes easily that the same holds for the kernel of (17), to which one may therefore apply the same argument, so that \(\mathfrak{F}\) is represented as the cokernel of a homomorphism
\[ \tag{18} \widehat{L'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widehat{L}, \]

where \(L\), \(L'\) are locally free Modules on \(X\). Now by virtue of a\('\)) and the second part of c\('\)), and of 2.1 or 2.4 as one chooses, the homomorphism (18) comes from a homomorphism \(L'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L\) of Modules on \(X\). It now suffices to take for \(F\) the cokernel of \(L'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L\), and we are done.

It remains to prove 3.2. This had been done in the seminar by a somewhat tedious expedient, consisting in interpreting everything in terms of cohomology on the punctured projecting cone of \(X\) relative to \(S\), in order to be able to reduce to theorem 2.1. A more direct and more satisfactory way (although substantially identical) now seems to me the following. It consists in noting that in IX, nº 2 (and with the notations of that exposé) the hypothesis that the morphism \(f:\mathscr{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{X}'\) be adic intervenes nowhere in the proof of 2.1, via \(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 13.7.7; it suffices to assume instead that \(\mathscr{X}\) is likewise adic, and to choose two ideals of definition \(\mathscr{J}\) for \(\mathscr{X}'\), \(\mathscr{I}\) for \(\mathscr{X}\), such that \(\mathscr{J}\mathscr{O}_\mathscr{X}\subset\mathscr{I}\), and to define \(\mathscr{S}=\operatorname{gr}_\mathscr{J}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'})\), and consider \(\operatorname{gr}_\mathscr{I}(\mathfrak{F})\). In any case, 2.1 can be applied directly to the morphism \(\widehat{f}:\widehat{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) considered in the present section, where one simply takes \(\mathscr{J}=0\). Thus to verify that \(\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F})\) is coherent, it suffices by virtue of loc. cit. to verify that \(\operatorname{R}^if_{0\ast}(\operatorname{gr}_\mathscr{I}(\mathfrak{F}))\) is coherent on \(S\) for \(i=0, 1\); for this one notes that by virtue of a) and b), the Module under consideration is none other than \(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\operatorname{R}^if_{0\ast}(F_0(-m))\), which is indeed coherent by virtue of hypothesis c) and of 1.5.
This proves 3.2 (i). For 3.2 (ii), we shall need the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3

Under conditions a), b), c) of 3.1, set

\[ G_m= \widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}(\cdot)_m) = \coprod_n\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}_m(n)) \]

Then the projective system \((G_m)\) satisfies the Mittag-Leffler condition.

We may assume \(S\) affine, with ring \(A\). Let then \(\mathscr{S}\) be a finitely generated graded \(A\)-algebra with positive degrees, and \(t'\in\mathscr{S}_1\), such that \(X\) embeds in \(\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S})\), \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) being induced by \(\mathbf{Proj}(\mathscr{S}(1))\) and the section \(t\) being the image of \(t'\). Endow \(\mathscr{S}\) with the \(\mathscr{J}\)-adic filtration, where \(\mathscr{J}=t'\mathscr{S}\), and consider the projective system of the \(\mathfrak{F}(\cdot)_m\) in the category of abelian sheaves on \(X_0\). We are again under the preliminary conditions of \(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 13.7.71 and moreover \(\operatorname{H}^i(X_0, \operatorname{gr}(\mathfrak{F}(\cdot))\) is a finitely generated Module over \(\operatorname{gr}_\mathscr{J}(\mathscr{S})\) for \(i=0, 1\). Indeed, since \(t'\) is \(\mathfrak{F}\)-regular, one immediately sees that as a Module over \((\mathscr{S} /t'\mathscr{S})[T]\) (of which \(\operatorname{gr}_\mathscr{J}(\mathscr{S})\) is a quotient), the Module under consideration is identified with \(\operatorname{H}^i(X_0, F_0(\cdot))\otimes_{\mathscr{S} /t'\mathscr{S}}(\mathscr{S} /t'\mathscr{S})[T]\), and by virtue of 1.5, \(\operatorname{H}^i(X_0, F_0(\cdot))\) is finitely generated over \(\mathscr{S}\), hence over \(\mathscr{S} /t'\mathscr{S}\), for \(i=0, 1\), which proves our assertion. Consequently we are under the conditions of application of \(0_{\text{III}}\) 13.7.7 with \(n=1\), which proves 3.3.

This point being acquired (and still assuming \(S\) affine, which is permissible for proving 3.2 (ii)) let \(m_0\) be such that \(m\geqslant m_0\) implies \(\operatorname{Im}(G_m\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0)= \operatorname{Im}(G_{m_0}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0)\), so that the two members are also equal to \(\operatorname{Im}(\varprojlim G_m\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_0) = \operatorname{Im}(\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}(\cdot))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_\ast\mathfrak{F}_0(\cdot))\). Let us now remark that for \(n\) large, \(\mathfrak{F}_{m_0}(n)\) is generated by its sections, hence \(\mathfrak{F}_0(n)\) is generated by sections that lift to \(\mathfrak{F}_{m_0}\), hence (thanks to the choice of \(m_0\)) that lift to \(\mathfrak{F}\). Hence the sections of \(\mathfrak{F}\) generate \(\mathfrak{F}_0\), hence also \(\mathfrak{F}\) thanks to Nakayama. This proves 3.2 (ii), hence 3.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a projective and flat morphism, with \(S\) locally noetherian, \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) an invertible Module on \(X\), ample relative to \(S\), \(t\) a section of this Module such that for every \(s\in S\), the section \(t_s\) induced on the fibre \(X_s\) is \(\mathscr{O}_{X_s}\)-regular, \(X_0\) the subprescheme of zeros of \(t\), \(\widehat{X}\) the formal completion of \(X\) along \(X_0\). We assume that for every \(s\in S\), \(X_{0s}\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at its closed points, (i.e. \(X_s\) has depth \(\geqslant 3\) at the closed points of \(X_{0s}\)) and \(X_s\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at its closed points. Under these conditions, the pair \((X, X_0)\) satisfies the effective Lefschetz condition (\(\operatorname{Leff}\)) of X 2, i.e.:

  1. For every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(X_0\) in \(X\), the functor

    \[ F \rightsquigarrow \widehat{F} \]

    from the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(U\) to the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(\widehat{X}\) is fully faithful.

  2. For every locally free coherent Module \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(\widehat{X}\), there exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(X_0\), and a locally free coherent Module \(F\) on \(U\) such that \(\mathfrak{F}\) is isomorphic to \(\widehat{F}\).

Indeed, a) has already been noted in 2.4 under weaker conditions. For b), one applies 3.1 which gives the conclusion, at least if \(S\) is noetherian and admits an absolutely ample invertible Module, in particular if \(S\) is affine. Indeed, if \(F\) is a coherent module on \(X\) such that \(\widehat{F}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathfrak{F}\) hence locally free, it follows that \(F\) is locally free on a neighbourhood \(U\) of \(X_0\), and \(F|U\) will satisfy the desired condition. But let us now note that by virtue of 2.5, for such an \(F\), its image under the immersion \(U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is coherent, and moreover independent of the chosen solution \((U, F)\) (given that two solutions coincide in a neighbourhood of \(X_0\), by virtue of a)). More precisely, one can find a coherent Module \(F\) on \(X\) and an isomorphism \(\widehat{F}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathfrak{F}\), such that \(F\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at all the points of \(X\) that are closed in their fibre, and this determines \(F\) up to a unique isomorphism. Thanks to this uniqueness property, the solutions of the problem found by inducing over the affine open sets of \(S\) glue together, whence an \(F\) coherent on all of \(X\) and an isomorphism \(\widehat{F}\simeq \mathfrak{F}\). Restricting \(F\) to the open set \(U\) of the points at which it is free, one finds what was sought.

Thanks to 2.4 and 3.4, one can exploit, in the situation of a projective algebraic scheme and of a "hyperplane section" thereof, the general facts established in exposés X and XI concerning the conditions (Lef) and (Leff). Thus:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5

Let \(X\) be a projective algebraic scheme equipped with an ample invertible Module \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\), let \(t\) be a section of this Module which is \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-regular, and let \(X_0\) be the subscheme of zeros of \(t\). Suppose that \(X\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at its closed points (resp. and depth \(\geqslant 3\) at the closed points of \(X_0\)). Then \(\pi_0(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0(X)\) is bijective, in particular \(X\) is connected if and only if \(X_0\) is, and choosing a geometric base point in \(X_0\), \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X)\) is surjective, and more generally for every open set \(U\supset X_0\), the homomorphism \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(U)\) is surjective (resp. the homomorphism \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_U\pi_1(U)\) is bijective). In the resp. case, if one assumes moreover that the local ring of every closed point of \(X\) not in \(X_0\) is pure (3.2) (for example is regular, or merely a complete intersection) then \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X)\) is an isomorphism.

One applies 2.4 and 3.4. One will note that in the resp. case, the hypothesis that \(X\) has depth \(\geqslant 3\) at the closed points of \(X_0\) implies that all the irreducible components of dimension \(\neq 0\) of \(X\) have dimension \(\geqslant 3\) (as one sees by noting that such a component necessarily meets \(X_0\), and by looking at a closed point of the intersection).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

When \(X\) is normal, of dimension \(\geqslant 2\) at all its points, it has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at its closed points and we are under the non-resp. conditions of 3.5. In this case, one has a more elementary proof of the surjectivity of \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X)\) using Bertini's theorem (cf. SGA 1 X.2.10). When one assumes moreover \(X_0\) normal, and \(X\) of dimension \(\geqslant 3\) at all its points, then we are under the resp. conditions of 3.5. In this case, 3.5 was established by Grauert (it turns out indeed that, thanks to the normality hypothesis, one then manages to dispense with the existence theorem 3.1 by expedients). It is this proof of Grauert that was the starting point of the "Lefschetz theory" which is the subject of the present seminar.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.6

Let \(X\), \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\), \(t\), \(X_0\) be as in 3.5. Suppose that \(X\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) at its closed points, and that

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X_0, \mathscr{O}_{X_0}(-n))=0 \]

for \(n>0\) and for \(i=1\) (resp. for \(i=1\) and for \(i=2\)), which implies by virtue of 1.4 that \(X_0\) has depth \(\geqslant 2\) (resp. \(\geqslant 3\)) at its closed points, i.e. that \(X\) has depth \(\geqslant 3\) (resp. \(\geqslant 4\)) at the closed points of \(X_0\). Under these conditions, for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(X_0\), \(\operatorname{Pic}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(X_0)\) is injective, in particular \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(X_0)\) is injective, (resp. \(\varprojlim_U \operatorname{Pic}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(X_0)\) is bijective). In the resp. case, if one assumes moreover that the local ring of \(X\) at every closed point not in \(X_0\) is parafactorial (3.1) (for example is regular, or more generally a complete intersection), then \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(X_0)\) is bijective.

One applies XI 3.12 and 3.13, noting that the resp. hypothesis implies that the irreducible components of dimension \(\neq 0\) of \(X\) have dimension \(\geqslant 4\). One finds in particular, applying this to the case where \(X\) is a global complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant 4\) in projective space:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7

Let \(X\) be an algebraic scheme of dimension \(\geqslant 3\), which is a complete intersection in a scheme \(\mathbf{P}_k^r\). Then \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) is the free group generated by the class of the sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\).

One argues by induction on the number of hypersurfaces of which \(X\) is the intersection, applying 3.6 and noting that for a complete intersection \(X\) of dimension \(\geqslant 3\), one has \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, \mathscr{O}_X(n))=0\) for \(i=1, 2\), and all \(n\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.8

In the case where \(X\) is a nonsingular hypersurface, 3.7 is due to Andreotti. The result 3.7 can also be expressed (when \(X\) is nonsingular) by saying that the homogeneous coordinate ring of \(X\) is factorial, and in this form is contained in XI 3.13 (ii). Let us also point out that Serre had given a proof of 3.7 in the nonsingular case, by transcendental means, using a specialization argument to reduce to the case of characteristic 0, where one has Lefschetz's theorem in its classical form. Of course, the fact that the purely algebraic proof given here makes it possible to get rid of nonsingularity hypotheses in the statement of Lefschetz's theorem invites one to reconsider the latter also in the classical case. Cf. the following exposé, which proposes conjectures in this direction.

In corollaries 3.5 and 3.7 we have placed ourselves over a base field, whereas the key theorems 2.4 and 3.4 are valid over an arbitrary base. To generalize these corollaries 3.5 and 3.6 to a general \(S\), we must give workable criteria for a point of \(X\) (flat over \(S\)) to have a "pure" resp. parafactorial local ring. This will be the subject of the next no.

footnotes.html: Corrected as indicated in IX p. 11

pages.title: Formal completion and normal flatness
pages.html:
4 Formal completion and normal flatness

Theorem 4.1

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, locally immersible in a regular scheme, \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\), \(U=X- Y\), \(X_0\) a closed subprescheme of \(X\) defined by an ideal \(\mathscr{J}\), \(\widehat{X}\) the formal completion of \(X\) along \(X_0\), \(U_0\) the trace of \(X_0\) on \(U\), \(\widehat{U}\) the formal completion of \(U\) along \(U_0\), \(i:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(\widehat{i}:\widehat{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widehat{X}\) the canonical immersions, \(n\) an integer. We suppose

  1. \(X\) is normally flat along \(X_0\) at the points of \(Y\cap X_0\), i.e. at these points the modules \(\mathscr{J}^n/\mathscr{J}^{n+1}\) over \(X_0\) are flat, i.e. free.

  2. For all \(x\in Y\cap X_0\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X_0, x}\geqslant n+2\).

Under these conditions, one has the following:

  1. \(1^\circ)\)

    Let \(F\) be a coherent Module on \(U\); suppose that one has:

    1. c)

      For all \(x\in Y- Y\cap X_0\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X, x}\geqslant n+2\).

    2. d)

      \(F\) is free at the points of \(U_0\), and of depth \(\geqslant n+1\) at all the points of \(U\) where it is not free.

    Then the graded Module

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(\mathscr{J}^mF) \]

    over \(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m\) is of finite type for \(p\leqslant n\).

  2. \(2^\circ\)

    Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be a coherent Module on \(\widehat{U}\); then the graded Module

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(\mathscr{J}^m\mathfrak{F}/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}\mathfrak{F}) \]

    over \(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}=\operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{J}}(\mathscr{O}_X)\) is of finite type for \(p\leqslant n\).

Proof▾

1) Let \(X'\!=\!\operatorname{Spec}(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m)\); the base change \(f:X'\!\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\! X\) then defines \(U'=X'- Y'\), \(X_0'\), \(U_0'=X_0'\cap U'\), and immersions \(i':U'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\), \(i_0':U_0'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_0'\). One thus has a cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix{ X^{\prime}\ar[d]_f&U^{\prime}\ar[l]_-{i^{\prime}}\ar[d]^g \\ X&U\ar[l]_-{i} } \]

and one has

\[ \tag{19} \coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(\mathscr{J}^mF) = \operatorname{R}^pi_\ast \Big(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{J}^mF \Big) =\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast (g_\ast(F')), \]

where \(F'=g^\ast F\), so that one does have a canonical isomorphism

\[ g_\ast(F') \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^mF, \]

for it is true at the points of \(U_0\), since \(F\) is free there by virtue of d), and likewise at the points of \(U_0\), since one has \(\mathscr{J}^m=\mathscr{O}_U\) there, (so that in both cases, \(\mathscr{J}^m\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_U}F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{J}^mF\) is an isomorphism).

On the other hand, since \(f\) and consequently \(g\) are affine, one has

\[ \tag{20} {}\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(g_\ast(F')) = \operatorname{R}^p(ig)_\ast(F') = \operatorname{R}^p(fi')_\ast(F') = f_\ast(R^pi'_\ast(F')) \]

hence comparing (19) and (20), one sees that assertion 1\({}^\circ\)) is equivalent to the following: \(R^pi'_\ast(F')\) is a Module of finite type, i.e. coherent on \(X'\), for all \(p\leqslant n\). Now since \(X\) is locally immersible in a regular scheme, the same holds for \(X'\), which is of finite type over \(X\), and one can apply the coherence criterion 2.3 to a coherent extension \(F^{''}\) of \(F'\): one wants to express that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^p_Y(F^{''})\) is coherent for \(p\leqslant n+1\), and this is also equivalent to saying that for all \(x'\in U'\) such that

\[ \tag{20 bis} {}\operatorname{codim} (\overline{x'}\cap Y', \overline{x'})=1, \]

one has

\[ \tag{21} {} \operatorname{prof} F'_{x'}\geqslant n+1. \]
