Now this condition is satisfied at the points \(x'\) where \(F'\) is not free, for at such an \(x'\) one has \(x'\notin U_0'\) by virtue of d), hence \(g\) is an isomorphism there, and again by virtue of d), \(F\) is of depth \(\geqslant n+1\) at \(g(x')\), hence \(F'\) is of depth \(\geqslant n+1\) at \(x'\). It therefore suffices to verify condition (21) at the \(x'\in U'\) satisfying (21), and at which \(F'\) is free. Now for this it suffices to prove that one has

\[ \tag{21 bis} \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X', x'}\geqslant n+1 \]

at these points; a fortiori it suffices to establish that one has this relation at all the points \(x\) of \(U'\) satisfying (20). Now by virtue of the cited criterion of Exposé VIII, this is equivalent to the assertion that the Modules

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^p_{Y'}(\mathscr{O}_{X'}) \hspace{5mm} \text{for} \; p\leqslant n+1 \]

are coherent. In fact, we shall prove that they are even zero, or what amounts to the same by virtue of Exposé III, that one has

\[ \tag{22} {} \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X', x'}\geqslant n+2 \hspace{5mm} \text{for all }\; x'\in Y'. \]

For this, we distinguish two cases. If \(x'\notin X'_0\), then \(f\) is an immersion at \(x'\), and one must verify that \(F\) is of depth in the image \(x=f(x')\), which is nothing but condition c). If on the contrary \(x'\in X'_0\), i.e. \(x=f(x')\in X_0\) hence \(x\in Y\cap X_0\), one applies conditions a) and b) by means of the

Lemma 4.2

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(X_0\) a closed subprescheme of \(X\) defined by an ideal \(\mathscr{J}\), \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{J}^m)\), \(X_0'=\operatorname{Spec}(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{J}^m/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}) = X'\times_XX_0\), \(x\) a point of \(X_0\) at which \(X\) is normally flat along \(X_0\), i.e. such that \(\operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{J}}(\mathscr{O}_X)= \coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}\) is flat there as a Module over \(X_0\). Then for any sequence of elements \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant m\)) of \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) whose images in \(\mathscr{O}_{X_0, x}\) form an \(\mathscr{O}_{X_0, x}\)-regular sequence, and any \(x'\in X'\) lying over \(x\), the images of the \(f_i\) in \(\mathscr{O}_{X', x'}\) (resp. in \(\mathscr{O}_{X'_0, x'}\)) likewise form an \(\mathscr{O}_{X', x'}\)-regular sequence (resp. \(\mathscr{O}_{X'_0, x'}\)-regular sequence); in particular one has

\[ \tag{23} \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X', x'} \geqslant \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X_0, x}, \quad \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X'_0, x'} \geqslant \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X_0, x}. \]

To prove it, one may suppose that \(X\) is local with closed point \(x\), hence affine with ring \(A=\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\), \(\mathscr{J}\) being defined by the Ideal \(J\), and it suffices to prove that for any sequence \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant m\)) of elements of \(A\) whose images in \(A/J\) form an \(A/J\)-regular sequence, the \(f_i\) likewise form a \((\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} J^m)\)-regular sequence and a \((\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} J^m/J^{m+1})\)-regular sequence, i.e. for all \(m\), it forms a \(J^m\)-regular and \(J^m/J^{m+1}\)-regular sequence. The second assertion is trivial, since \(J^m/J^{m+1}\) is a free module over \(A/J\). The first follows, by looking at the \(J\)-adic filtration of \(J^m\), and noting that for the graded module associated to \(J^m\) for the said filtration, the sequence of the \(f_i\) is regular.

This proves 4.2 and consequently 4.1 1).

Let us prove 4.1 2). For this, let us use the cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix{ X^{\prime}_0\ar[d]_{f_0}&U^{\prime}_0\ar[l]_-{i^{\prime}_0}\ar[d]^{g_0} \\ X_0&U_0\ar[l]_-{i_0} } \]

and proceeding as in the beginning of the proof of 1), one finds that one has

\[ \tag{24} \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(\mathscr{J}^m\mathfrak{F}/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}\mathfrak{F}) \simeq {f_0}_\ast (\operatorname{R}^p{i_0}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}_0')), \]

where \(\mathfrak{F}_0=\mathfrak{F}/\mathscr{J}\mathfrak{F}\) and where \(\mathfrak{F}_0'=g_0^\ast(\mathfrak{F}_0)\), (using the fact that \(\mathfrak{F}\) is locally free). Hence the conclusion of 2) is equivalent to saying that for \(p\leqslant n\), \(\operatorname{R}^p{i'_0}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}_0')\) is a coherent Module.

Here again, given that \(\mathfrak{F}_0'\) is locally free, the criterion VIII 2.3 allows us to reduce to proving that this is so if one replaces \(\mathfrak{F}_0'\) by \(\mathscr{O}_{U'_0}\), i.e. to proving that the Modules

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^p_{Y_0'}(\mathscr{O}_{X_0'}) \hspace{5mm} \text{for} \; p\leqslant n+1 \hspace{5mm} \text{(where} \; Y'_0=Y'\cap X_0'=X_0'- U'_0 \text{)} \]

are coherent. One proves once more that they are in fact zero, i.e. that one has

\[ \tag{25} {}\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X'_0, x'} \geqslant n+2 \hspace{5mm} \text{for all} \; x'\in Y'_0. \]

Now this indeed results from conditions a) and b), taking 4.2 into account. This completes the proof of 4.1.

Q.E.D.
Remark 4.3

One sees at once, by descent, that the hypothesis: \(X\) locally immersible in a regular scheme, can be replaced by the following weaker one: there exists a morphism \(\overline{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), faithfully flat and quasi-compact, such that \(\overline{X}\) is locally immersible in a regular scheme.

Theorem 4.1 puts us in a position to apply the results of Exposé IX (comparison and existence theorems). We shall be interested in particular in the

Corollary 4.4

Suppose conditions a), b), c) of Theorem 4.1 are satisfied, with \(n=1\), and \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(A\) being separated and complete for the \(J\)-adic topology. Then

  1. 1)

    The functor \(F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \widehat{F}\) from the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(U\), to the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(\widehat{U}\), is fully faithful.

  2. 2)

    For every locally free coherent Module \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(\widehat{U}\), there exist a coherent Module \(F\) on \(U\) and an isomorphism \(\widehat{F}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathfrak{F}\).

In particular, if for every \(x\in U\) whose closure in \(U\) does not meet \(U_0\), i.e. such that \(\overline{x}\cap X_0\subset Y\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{U, x}\geqslant 2\), then the pair \((U, U_0)\) satisfies the effective Lefschetz condition (\(\operatorname{Leff}\)) of Exposé X.

(For the last assertion, one proceeds as in X 2.1)

[2] Particular case of 4.4:

Corollary 4.5

Let \(A\) be a noetherian ring, \(J\) an ideal of \(A\) contained in the radical, \(A_0=A/J\). We suppose

  1. \(\operatorname{prof} A_0\geqslant 3\).

  2. \(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\) is a free \(A_0\)-module.

  3. \(A\) is complete for the \(J\)-adic topology.

Let \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(X_0=\operatorname{Spec}(A_0)=V(J)\), \(a\) the closed point of \(X\), \(U=X-\{a\}\), \(U_0=X_0-\{a\}\), \(\widehat{U}\) the formal completion of \(U\) along \(U_0\). Then the functor \(F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \widehat{F}\) from the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(U\) to the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(\widehat{U}\), is fully faithful. Moreover, for every locally free coherent Module \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(\widehat{U}\), there exist a coherent Module (not necessarily locally free!) \(F\) on \(U\), and an isomorphism \(\widehat{F}\simeq\mathfrak{F}\).

One will note that thanks to 4.3, we did not have to suppose that \(A\) is a quotient of a regular ring, for the completion of \(A\) for the \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\)-adic topology satisfies this condition in any case.

Proceeding as in Exposés X and XI, one concludes from 4.5

Corollary 4.6

Under the conditions of 4.5, one has the following:

  1. \(U\) and \(U_0\) are connected (III 3.1).

    Choosing a geometric base point in \(U_0\), the homomorphism

    \[ \pi_1(U_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(U) \]

    is surjective.

  2. The homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{Pic}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(U_0) \]

    is injective.

To prove b), taking 4.5 into account, this amounts to verifying that every isomorphism \(L_0'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} L_0\) lifts to an isomorphism \(\widehat{L'}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widehat{L}\). Now for this one lifts step by step to isomorphisms \(L_n'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} L_n\); the obstructions lie in \(\operatorname{H}^1(U_0, \mathscr{J}^n/\mathscr{J}^{n+1})\), and these modules are zero since \(J^n/J^{n+1}\) is free and \(\operatorname{prof} A_0\geqslant 3\).

We are now in a position to prove the -1

Theorem 4.7

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring, \(J\) an ideal of \(A\) contained in its radical, \(A_0=A/J\). We suppose

  1. \(\operatorname{prof} A_0\geqslant 3\).

  2. \(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\) is a free module over \(A_0\).

Then, if \(A_0\) is "pure" (X 3.1) (resp. parafactorial (XI 3.1)), the same holds for \(A\).

Proof▾

By descent, one may suppose that one also has

  1. \(A\) is complete for the \(J\)-adic topology.

Indeed, by virtue of (i) and (ii), one has \(\operatorname{prof}(A)\geqslant 3\) hence \(\operatorname{prof}(\widehat{A})\geqslant 3\), where \(\widehat{A}\) is the completion of \(A\) for the \(J\)-adic topology, and one applies X 3.6 and XI 3.6. One is thus under the conditions of 4.5. Since \(\operatorname{prof}(A)\geqslant 3\geqslant 2\), saying that \(A\) is parafactorial simply means that \(\operatorname{Pic}(U)=0\), and by virtue of 4.6 b) it suffices for this that \(\operatorname{Pic}(U_0)=0\), i.e. that \(A_0\) be parafactorial. To prove that \(A\) is "pure" if \(A_0\) is, one must prove that if \(V\) is an étale covering of \(U\), defined by an algebra \(\mathscr{B}\) over \(U\), then \(\operatorname{H}^0(U, \mathscr{B})\) is a finite étale algebra over \(A\). Now \(A_0\) being pure, the same holds for the \(A_n\) (which differ from it only by nilpotent elements), hence for all \(n\), \(\operatorname{H}^0(U, \mathscr{B}_n)\) is an étale algebra over \(A/J^{n+1}\), and of course these algebras glue together, so that \(\varprojlim B_n\) is an étale algebra over \(A\). Now by virtue of 4.5, this algebra is none other than \(\operatorname{H}^0(U, \mathscr{B})\), which establishes our assertion.

Q.E.D.
Corollary 4.8

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a flat morphism of locally noetherian preschemes, \(x\in X\), \(y=f(x)\); suppose that \(\mathscr{O}_{X_y, x}\) is a "pure" (resp. parafactorial) local ring of depth \(\geqslant 3\); then the same holds for \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\).

This is the result of the type promised at the end of the preceding No, to generalize corollaries 3.5 and following. One thus finds, using 3.4, the

Corollary 4.9

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a projective and flat morphism, with \(S\) locally noetherian, \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) an invertible Module on \(X\) ample relative to \(S\), \(t\) a section of \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) such that for all \(s\in S\), the section \(t_s\) induced on \(X_s\) is \(\mathscr{O}_{X_s}\)-regular, \(X_0\) the subscheme of zeros of \(t\), \(X_m\) the subprescheme of zeros of \(t^{m+1}\). Suppose that for all \(s\in S\), \(X_s\) is of depth \(\geqslant 3\) at all its closed points. Then:

  1. If the local rings of the closed points of \(X_s- X_{0, s}\) (\(s\in S\)) are "pure", for example are complete intersections, then the functor \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X_0'=X'\times_X X_0\) from the category of étale coverings of \(X\) to the category of étale coverings of \(X_0\) is an equivalence of categories; in particular, choosing a geometric base point in \(X_0\), the homomorphism

    \[ \pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X) \]

    is an isomorphism

  2. If the local rings of the closed points of the \(X_s- X_{0, s}\) (\(s\in S\)) are "parafactorial", for example regular, or complete intersections of dimension \(\geqslant 4\), then for every integer \(m\) such that \(\operatorname{R}^if_{0\ast}(\mathscr{O}_{X_0}(-n))=0\) for \(n>m\) and \(i=1, 2\), the map \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(X_m)\) is bijective.

    Moreover, if \(S\) is noetherian, and the \( X_{0, s}\) are of depth \(\geqslant 3\) at their closed points, there exist such \(m\) (cf. 1.5).

Remark 4.10

Under the conditions of the last assertion of 4.9 b), one saw in 1.5 that there exists an \(m\) such that \(n>m\) even implies \(\operatorname{H}^i(X_0, \mathscr{O}_{X_0}(-n))=0\) for \(i=1, 2\), and even for \(i\leqslant 2\)). This condition is stronger than \(\operatorname{R}^if_{\ast}(\mathscr{O}_{X_0}(-n))=0\) for \(i=1, 2\), and it has moreover the advantage of being stable under base change. The same holds for the depth hypotheses made in 4.9, and likewise for a hypothesis of the type "the \(X_s\) are locally complete intersections". It then follows, under these conditions, that 4.9 b) also implies that the morphism of functors

\[ \mathbf{Pic}_{X/S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Pic}_{X_n/S} \]

in \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\) is an isomorphism, hence so is the morphism for the relative Picard schemes, when these exist:

\[ \operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}_{X_n/S}. \]

Even in the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field, this statement is markedly more precise than the statement consisting in saying merely that \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(X_n)\) is bijective.

One may ask whether one can always take \(n=0\) in the preceding conclusions (thus supposing the \(X_{0, s}\) of depth \(\geqslant 3\) at their closed points). When \(X_0\) is smooth over \(S\) and the residue characteristics of \(S\) are zero, this is indeed so, by virtue of Kodaira's "vanishing theorem", (proved by transcendental means, using a Kählerian metric) which implies that for every smooth connected projective scheme of dimension \(n\) over a field \(k\) of characteristic zero, and every ample invertible Module \(L\) on \(X\), one has \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, L^{-1})=0\) for \(i\neq n\). It is not known at present whether this theorem can be replaced by a generalization in characteristic \(p>0\), and whether the smoothness hypothesis can be replaced by a hypothesis of a more general nature (bearing on the depth, or of the "complete intersection" type...).

blocks.html: 4 Formal completion and normal flatness

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, locally immersible in a regular scheme, \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\), \(U=X- Y\), \(X_0\) a closed subprescheme of \(X\) defined by an ideal \(\mathscr{J}\), \(\widehat{X}\) the formal completion of \(X\) along \(X_0\), \(U_0\) the trace of \(X_0\) on \(U\), \(\widehat{U}\) the formal completion of \(U\) along \(U_0\), \(i:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(\widehat{i}:\widehat{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widehat{X}\) the canonical immersions, \(n\) an integer. We suppose

  1. \(X\) is normally flat along \(X_0\) at the points of \(Y\cap X_0\), i.e. at these points the modules \(\mathscr{J}^n/\mathscr{J}^{n+1}\) over \(X_0\) are flat, i.e. free.

  2. For all \(x\in Y\cap X_0\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X_0, x}\geqslant n+2\).

Under these conditions, one has the following:

  1. \(1^\circ)\)

    Let \(F\) be a coherent Module on \(U\); suppose that one has:

    1. c)

      For all \(x\in Y- Y\cap X_0\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X, x}\geqslant n+2\).

    2. d)

      \(F\) is free at the points of \(U_0\), and of depth \(\geqslant n+1\) at all the points of \(U\) where it is not free.

    Then the graded Module

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(\mathscr{J}^mF) \]

    over \(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m\) is of finite type for \(p\leqslant n\).

  2. \(2^\circ\)

    Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be a coherent Module on \(\widehat{U}\); then the graded Module

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(\mathscr{J}^m\mathfrak{F}/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}\mathfrak{F}) \]

    over \(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}=\operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{J}}(\mathscr{O}_X)\) is of finite type for \(p\leqslant n\).

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

1) Let \(X'\!=\!\operatorname{Spec}(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m)\); the base change \(f:X'\!\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\! X\) then defines \(U'=X'- Y'\), \(X_0'\), \(U_0'=X_0'\cap U'\), and immersions \(i':U'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\), \(i_0':U_0'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_0'\). One thus has a cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix{ X^{\prime}\ar[d]_f&U^{\prime}\ar[l]_-{i^{\prime}}\ar[d]^g \\ X&U\ar[l]_-{i} } \]

and one has

\[ \tag{19} \coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(\mathscr{J}^mF) = \operatorname{R}^pi_\ast \Big(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{J}^mF \Big) =\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast (g_\ast(F')), \]

where \(F'=g^\ast F\), so that one does have a canonical isomorphism

\[ g_\ast(F') \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^mF, \]

for it is true at the points of \(U_0\), since \(F\) is free there by virtue of d), and likewise at the points of \(U_0\), since one has \(\mathscr{J}^m=\mathscr{O}_U\) there, (so that in both cases, \(\mathscr{J}^m\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_U}F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{J}^mF\) is an isomorphism).

On the other hand, since \(f\) and consequently \(g\) are affine, one has

\[ \tag{20} {}\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(g_\ast(F')) = \operatorname{R}^p(ig)_\ast(F') = \operatorname{R}^p(fi')_\ast(F') = f_\ast(R^pi'_\ast(F')) \]

hence comparing (19) and (20), one sees that assertion 1\({}^\circ\)) is equivalent to the following: \(R^pi'_\ast(F')\) is a Module of finite type, i.e. coherent on \(X'\), for all \(p\leqslant n\). Now since \(X\) is locally immersible in a regular scheme, the same holds for \(X'\), which is of finite type over \(X\), and one can apply the coherence criterion 2.3 to a coherent extension \(F^{''}\) of \(F'\): one wants to express that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^p_Y(F^{''})\) is coherent for \(p\leqslant n+1\), and this is also equivalent to saying that for all \(x'\in U'\) such that

\[ \tag{20 bis} {}\operatorname{codim} (\overline{x'}\cap Y', \overline{x'})=1, \]

one has

\[ \tag{21} {} \operatorname{prof} F'_{x'}\geqslant n+1. \]

Now this condition is satisfied at the points \(x'\) where \(F'\) is not free, for at such an \(x'\) one has \(x'\notin U_0'\) by virtue of d), hence \(g\) is an isomorphism there, and again by virtue of d), \(F\) is of depth \(\geqslant n+1\) at \(g(x')\), hence \(F'\) is of depth \(\geqslant n+1\) at \(x'\). It therefore suffices to verify condition (21) at the \(x'\in U'\) satisfying (21), and at which \(F'\) is free. Now for this it suffices to prove that one has

\[ \tag{21 bis} \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X', x'}\geqslant n+1 \]

at these points; a fortiori it suffices to establish that one has this relation at all the points \(x\) of \(U'\) satisfying (20). Now by virtue of the cited criterion of Exposé VIII, this is equivalent to the assertion that the Modules

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^p_{Y'}(\mathscr{O}_{X'}) \hspace{5mm} \text{for} \; p\leqslant n+1 \]

are coherent. In fact, we shall prove that they are even zero, or what amounts to the same by virtue of Exposé III, that one has

\[ \tag{22} {} \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X', x'}\geqslant n+2 \hspace{5mm} \text{for all }\; x'\in Y'. \]

For this, we distinguish two cases. If \(x'\notin X'_0\), then \(f\) is an immersion at \(x'\), and one must verify that \(F\) is of depth in the image \(x=f(x')\), which is nothing but condition c). If on the contrary \(x'\in X'_0\), i.e. \(x=f(x')\in X_0\) hence \(x\in Y\cap X_0\), one applies conditions a) and b) by means of the

Lemma 4.2

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(X_0\) a closed subprescheme of \(X\) defined by an ideal \(\mathscr{J}\), \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{J}^m)\), \(X_0'=\operatorname{Spec}(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{J}^m/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}) = X'\times_XX_0\), \(x\) a point of \(X_0\) at which \(X\) is normally flat along \(X_0\), i.e. such that \(\operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{J}}(\mathscr{O}_X)= \coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}\) is flat there as a Module over \(X_0\). Then for any sequence of elements \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant m\)) of \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) whose images in \(\mathscr{O}_{X_0, x}\) form an \(\mathscr{O}_{X_0, x}\)-regular sequence, and any \(x'\in X'\) lying over \(x\), the images of the \(f_i\) in \(\mathscr{O}_{X', x'}\) (resp. in \(\mathscr{O}_{X'_0, x'}\)) likewise form an \(\mathscr{O}_{X', x'}\)-regular sequence (resp. \(\mathscr{O}_{X'_0, x'}\)-regular sequence); in particular one has

\[ \tag{23} \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X', x'} \geqslant \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X_0, x}, \quad \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X'_0, x'} \geqslant \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X_0, x}. \]

To prove it, one may suppose that \(X\) is local with closed point \(x\), hence affine with ring \(A=\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\), \(\mathscr{J}\) being defined by the Ideal \(J\), and it suffices to prove that for any sequence \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant m\)) of elements of \(A\) whose images in \(A/J\) form an \(A/J\)-regular sequence, the \(f_i\) likewise form a \((\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} J^m)\)-regular sequence and a \((\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} J^m/J^{m+1})\)-regular sequence, i.e. for all \(m\), it forms a \(J^m\)-regular and \(J^m/J^{m+1}\)-regular sequence. The second assertion is trivial, since \(J^m/J^{m+1}\) is a free module over \(A/J\). The first follows, by looking at the \(J\)-adic filtration of \(J^m\), and noting that for the graded module associated to \(J^m\) for the said filtration, the sequence of the \(f_i\) is regular.

This proves 4.2 and consequently 4.1 1).

Let us prove 4.1 2). For this, let us use the cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix{ X^{\prime}_0\ar[d]_{f_0}&U^{\prime}_0\ar[l]_-{i^{\prime}_0}\ar[d]^{g_0} \\ X_0&U_0\ar[l]_-{i_0} } \]

and proceeding as in the beginning of the proof of 1), one finds that one has

\[ \tag{24} \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(\mathscr{J}^m\mathfrak{F}/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}\mathfrak{F}) \simeq {f_0}_\ast (\operatorname{R}^p{i_0}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}_0')), \]

where \(\mathfrak{F}_0=\mathfrak{F}/\mathscr{J}\mathfrak{F}\) and where \(\mathfrak{F}_0'=g_0^\ast(\mathfrak{F}_0)\), (using the fact that \(\mathfrak{F}\) is locally free). Hence the conclusion of 2) is equivalent to saying that for \(p\leqslant n\), \(\operatorname{R}^p{i'_0}_\ast(\mathfrak{F}_0')\) is a coherent Module.

Here again, given that \(\mathfrak{F}_0'\) is locally free, the criterion VIII 2.3 allows us to reduce to proving that this is so if one replaces \(\mathfrak{F}_0'\) by \(\mathscr{O}_{U'_0}\), i.e. to proving that the Modules

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^p_{Y_0'}(\mathscr{O}_{X_0'}) \hspace{5mm} \text{for} \; p\leqslant n+1 \hspace{5mm} \text{(where} \; Y'_0=Y'\cap X_0'=X_0'- U'_0 \text{)} \]

are coherent. One proves once more that they are in fact zero, i.e. that one has

\[ \tag{25} {}\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X'_0, x'} \geqslant n+2 \hspace{5mm} \text{for all} \; x'\in Y'_0. \]

Now this indeed results from conditions a) and b), taking 4.2 into account. This completes the proof of 4.1.

Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.3

One sees at once, by descent, that the hypothesis: \(X\) locally immersible in a regular scheme, can be replaced by the following weaker one: there exists a morphism \(\overline{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), faithfully flat and quasi-compact, such that \(\overline{X}\) is locally immersible in a regular scheme.
Theorem 4.1 puts us in a position to apply the results of Exposé IX (comparison and existence theorems). We shall be interested in particular in the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4

Suppose conditions a), b), c) of Theorem 4.1 are satisfied, with \(n=1\), and \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(A\) being separated and complete for the \(J\)-adic topology. Then

  1. 1)

    The functor \(F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \widehat{F}\) from the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(U\), to the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(\widehat{U}\), is fully faithful.

  2. 2)

    For every locally free coherent Module \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(\widehat{U}\), there exist a coherent Module \(F\) on \(U\) and an isomorphism \(\widehat{F}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathfrak{F}\).

In particular, if for every \(x\in U\) whose closure in \(U\) does not meet \(U_0\), i.e. such that \(\overline{x}\cap X_0\subset Y\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{U, x}\geqslant 2\), then the pair \((U, U_0)\) satisfies the effective Lefschetz condition (\(\operatorname{Leff}\)) of Exposé X.
(For the last assertion, one proceeds as in X 2.1)
[2] Particular case of 4.4:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5

Let \(A\) be a noetherian ring, \(J\) an ideal of \(A\) contained in the radical, \(A_0=A/J\). We suppose

  1. \(\operatorname{prof} A_0\geqslant 3\).

  2. \(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\) is a free \(A_0\)-module.

  3. \(A\) is complete for the \(J\)-adic topology.

Let \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(X_0=\operatorname{Spec}(A_0)=V(J)\), \(a\) the closed point of \(X\), \(U=X-\{a\}\), \(U_0=X_0-\{a\}\), \(\widehat{U}\) the formal completion of \(U\) along \(U_0\). Then the functor \(F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \widehat{F}\) from the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(U\) to the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(\widehat{U}\), is fully faithful. Moreover, for every locally free coherent Module \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(\widehat{U}\), there exist a coherent Module (not necessarily locally free!) \(F\) on \(U\), and an isomorphism \(\widehat{F}\simeq\mathfrak{F}\).

One will note that thanks to 4.3, we did not have to suppose that \(A\) is a quotient of a regular ring, for the completion of \(A\) for the \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\)-adic topology satisfies this condition in any case.
Proceeding as in Exposés X and XI, one concludes from 4.5

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6

Under the conditions of 4.5, one has the following:

  1. \(U\) and \(U_0\) are connected (III 3.1).

    Choosing a geometric base point in \(U_0\), the homomorphism

    \[ \pi_1(U_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(U) \]

    is surjective.

  2. The homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{Pic}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(U_0) \]

    is injective.

To prove b), taking 4.5 into account, this amounts to verifying that every isomorphism \(L_0'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} L_0\) lifts to an isomorphism \(\widehat{L'}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widehat{L}\). Now for this one lifts step by step to isomorphisms \(L_n'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} L_n\); the obstructions lie in \(\operatorname{H}^1(U_0, \mathscr{J}^n/\mathscr{J}^{n+1})\), and these modules are zero since \(J^n/J^{n+1}\) is free and \(\operatorname{prof} A_0\geqslant 3\).
We are now in a position to prove the -1

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.7

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring, \(J\) an ideal of \(A\) contained in its radical, \(A_0=A/J\). We suppose

  1. \(\operatorname{prof} A_0\geqslant 3\).

  2. \(\operatorname{gr}_J(A)\) is a free module over \(A_0\).

Then, if \(A_0\) is "pure" (X 3.1) (resp. parafactorial (XI 3.1)), the same holds for \(A\).

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

By descent, one may suppose that one also has

  1. \(A\) is complete for the \(J\)-adic topology.

Indeed, by virtue of (i) and (ii), one has \(\operatorname{prof}(A)\geqslant 3\) hence \(\operatorname{prof}(\widehat{A})\geqslant 3\), where \(\widehat{A}\) is the completion of \(A\) for the \(J\)-adic topology, and one applies X 3.6 and XI 3.6. One is thus under the conditions of 4.5. Since \(\operatorname{prof}(A)\geqslant 3\geqslant 2\), saying that \(A\) is parafactorial simply means that \(\operatorname{Pic}(U)=0\), and by virtue of 4.6 b) it suffices for this that \(\operatorname{Pic}(U_0)=0\), i.e. that \(A_0\) be parafactorial. To prove that \(A\) is "pure" if \(A_0\) is, one must prove that if \(V\) is an étale covering of \(U\), defined by an algebra \(\mathscr{B}\) over \(U\), then \(\operatorname{H}^0(U, \mathscr{B})\) is a finite étale algebra over \(A\). Now \(A_0\) being pure, the same holds for the \(A_n\) (which differ from it only by nilpotent elements), hence for all \(n\), \(\operatorname{H}^0(U, \mathscr{B}_n)\) is an étale algebra over \(A/J^{n+1}\), and of course these algebras glue together, so that \(\varprojlim B_n\) is an étale algebra over \(A\). Now by virtue of 4.5, this algebra is none other than \(\operatorname{H}^0(U, \mathscr{B})\), which establishes our assertion.

Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.8

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a flat morphism of locally noetherian preschemes, \(x\in X\), \(y=f(x)\); suppose that \(\mathscr{O}_{X_y, x}\) is a "pure" (resp. parafactorial) local ring of depth \(\geqslant 3\); then the same holds for \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\).
This is the result of the type promised at the end of the preceding No, to generalize corollaries 3.5 and following. One thus finds, using 3.4, the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.9

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a projective and flat morphism, with \(S\) locally noetherian, \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) an invertible Module on \(X\) ample relative to \(S\), \(t\) a section of \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) such that for all \(s\in S\), the section \(t_s\) induced on \(X_s\) is \(\mathscr{O}_{X_s}\)-regular, \(X_0\) the subscheme of zeros of \(t\), \(X_m\) the subprescheme of zeros of \(t^{m+1}\). Suppose that for all \(s\in S\), \(X_s\) is of depth \(\geqslant 3\) at all its closed points. Then:

  1. If the local rings of the closed points of \(X_s- X_{0, s}\) (\(s\in S\)) are "pure", for example are complete intersections, then the functor \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X_0'=X'\times_X X_0\) from the category of étale coverings of \(X\) to the category of étale coverings of \(X_0\) is an equivalence of categories; in particular, choosing a geometric base point in \(X_0\), the homomorphism

    \[ \pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X) \]

    is an isomorphism

  2. If the local rings of the closed points of the \(X_s- X_{0, s}\) (\(s\in S\)) are "parafactorial", for example regular, or complete intersections of dimension \(\geqslant 4\), then for every integer \(m\) such that \(\operatorname{R}^if_{0\ast}(\mathscr{O}_{X_0}(-n))=0\) for \(n>m\) and \(i=1, 2\), the map \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(X_m)\) is bijective.

    Moreover, if \(S\) is noetherian, and the \( X_{0, s}\) are of depth \(\geqslant 3\) at their closed points, there exist such \(m\) (cf. 1.5).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.10

Under the conditions of the last assertion of 4.9 b), one saw in 1.5 that there exists an \(m\) such that \(n>m\) even implies \(\operatorname{H}^i(X_0, \mathscr{O}_{X_0}(-n))=0\) for \(i=1, 2\), and even for \(i\leqslant 2\)). This condition is stronger than \(\operatorname{R}^if_{\ast}(\mathscr{O}_{X_0}(-n))=0\) for \(i=1, 2\), and it has moreover the advantage of being stable under base change. The same holds for the depth hypotheses made in 4.9, and likewise for a hypothesis of the type "the \(X_s\) are locally complete intersections". It then follows, under these conditions, that 4.9 b) also implies that the morphism of functors

\[ \mathbf{Pic}_{X/S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Pic}_{X_n/S} \]

in \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\) is an isomorphism, hence so is the morphism for the relative Picard schemes, when these exist:

\[ \operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}_{X_n/S}. \]

Even in the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field, this statement is markedly more precise than the statement consisting in saying merely that \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(X_n)\) is bijective.

One may ask whether one can always take \(n=0\) in the preceding conclusions (thus supposing the \(X_{0, s}\) of depth \(\geqslant 3\) at their closed points). When \(X_0\) is smooth over \(S\) and the residue characteristics of \(S\) are zero, this is indeed so, by virtue of Kodaira's "vanishing theorem", (proved by transcendental means, using a Kählerian metric) which implies that for every smooth connected projective scheme of dimension \(n\) over a field \(k\) of characteristic zero, and every ample invertible Module \(L\) on \(X\), one has \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, L^{-1})=0\) for \(i\neq n\). It is not known at present whether this theorem can be replaced by a generalization in characteristic \(p>0\), and whether the smoothness hypothesis can be replaced by a hypothesis of a more general nature (bearing on the depth, or of the "complete intersection" type...).

pages.title: Universal finiteness conditions for a non-proper morphism
pages.html:
5 Universal finiteness conditions for a non-proper morphism

Let us recall for the record the

Proposition 5.1

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper morphism of preschemes, with \(S\) locally noetherian, \(U\) an open part of \(X\), \(g:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion, \(h=fg:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), \(F\) a Module on \(U\). Suppose that the Modules \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) are coherent for \(i\leqslant n\) (a hypothesis of local nature on \(X\), which is verified in practice by means of the criterion VIII 2.3). Then \(\operatorname{R}^ih_\ast(F)\) is coherent for \(i\leqslant n\).

This follows immediately from the Leray spectral sequence

\[ \tag{26} {} \operatorname{E}_2^{p, q}=\operatorname{R}^pf_\ast(\operatorname{R}^qg_\ast(F)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \operatorname{R}^\ast h_\ast(F), \]

and from the fact that the higher direct images under \(f\) of a coherent Module on \(X\) are coherent (EGA III 3.2.1).

Proposition 5.2

Let \(S\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(\mathscr{S}\) a quasi-coherent graded Algebra, of finite type over \(S\), generated by \(\mathscr{S}_1\), \(X\) a sub-prescheme of \(\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S})\), \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) the invertible Module on \(X\) very ample relatively to \(S\) induced by \(\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S}(1))\), \(U\) an open part of \(X\), \(g:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion, \(h=fg:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), \(F\) a quasi-coherent Module on \(U\), whence twisted Modules \(F(m)=F\otimes \mathscr{O}_X(m)\)) (\(m\in\mathbf{Z}\)), \(n\) an integer, \(m_0\) an integer. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) is coherent for \(i\leqslant n\).

  2. \(\coprod_{m>m_0}\operatorname{R}^ih_\ast(F(m))\) is an \(\mathscr{S}\)-Module of finite type for \(i\leqslant n\).

Proof▾

Replacing \(F\) by \(F(m)\) in the spectral sequence above, one finds a spectral sequence of graded \(\mathscr{S}\)-Modules

\[ \operatorname{E}_2^{p, q}= \coprod_{m\geqslant m_0} \operatorname{R}^pf_\ast(\operatorname{R}^qg_\ast(F(m)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \coprod_{m\geqslant m_0} \operatorname{R}^\ast h_\ast(F(m)). \]

Since one has

\[ \operatorname{R}^qg_\ast(F(m)) \simeq \operatorname{R}^qg_\ast(F) (m), \]

one sees that if the \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) are coherent, \(\operatorname{E}_2^{p, q}\) is of finite type over \(\mathscr{S}\) for \(q\leqslant n\), thanks to part a) of lemma 5.3 below, which implies that the abutment is of finite type over \(\mathscr{S}\) in degree \(i\leqslant n\). This proves (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Moreover, reasoning in the abelian category of graded \(\mathscr{S}\)-Modules modulo the thick subcategory \(\mathscr{C}\) of those which are quasi-coherent of finite type, one finds by the preceding spectral sequence

\[ \coprod_{m\geqslant m_0} \operatorname{R}^{n+1} h_\ast(F(m)) \simeq \coprod_{m\geqslant m_0} f_\ast (\operatorname{R}^{n+1} g_\ast(F)(m)) \mod \mathscr{C}, \]

which proves that if the left-hand side is an \(\mathscr{S}\)-Module of finite type, then \(\operatorname{R}^{n+1} g_\ast(F)\) is coherent, by virtue of part b) of lemma 5.3. This proves the implication (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) by induction on \(n\). It remains to prove:

Lemma 5.3

Let \(S\), \(\mathscr{S}\), \(X\), \(f\) be as in 5.2, and \(G\) a quasi-coherent Module on \(X\), \(m_0\) an integer. Then

  1. If \(G\) is coherent, then for every integer \(i\), the graded Module

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant m_0} \operatorname{R}^i f_\ast(G(m)) \]

    over \(\mathscr{S}\) is of finite type.

  2. Conversely, suppose that the Module \(\coprod_{m\geqslant m_0} \operatorname{R}^i f_\ast(G(m))\) over \(\mathscr{S}\) is of finite type, then \(G\) is coherent.

Proof of 5.3▾

For a), the case \(i=0\) is given in EGA III 2.3.2, the case \(i>0\) in EGA III 2.2.1 (i)(ii) which says that the \( \operatorname{R}^i f_\ast G(m)\) are coherent, and zero for \(m\) large (if one assumes \(S\) noetherian, which is permissible). For b), one notes that \(G\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Proj} (\coprod_{m\geqslant m_0}f_\ast(G(m))\) (EGA II 3.4.4 and 3.4.2), which proves that \(G\) is coherent if \(\coprod_{m\geqslant m_0}f_\ast(G(m))\) is of finite type over \(\mathscr{S}\), by virtue of loc. cit. 3.4.4.

Q.E.D.
Q.E.D.
Corollary 5.4 (\(S\) noetherian)

Suppose that \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) is coherent for \(i\leqslant n\), then for \(i\leqslant n+1\), and \(m\) large, one has a canonical isomorphism:

\[ \operatorname{R}^ih_\ast(F(m)) \simeq f_\ast(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(G)(m)). \]

Indeed the spectral sequence (26) for \(F(m)\) then degenerates in degree \(\leqslant n\), by EGA III 2.2.1 (ii), whence the result immediately (which moreover gives back the implication (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) of 5.2).

1.2

Corollary 5.5

Under the preliminary conditions of 5.2, \(S\) noetherian, the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\coprod_{m\geqslant m_0}h_\ast(F(m)\) is of finite type over \(S\), and \(\operatorname{R}^ih_\ast(F(m))=0\) for \(0<i\leqslant n\) and \(m\) large.

  2. \(g_\ast(F)\) is coherent, and \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)=0\) for \(0<i\leqslant n\).

  3. (ii bis)

    \(g_\ast(F)\) is coherent, and \(\operatorname{prof}_Y g_\ast(F)>n+1\).

The equivalence of (ii) and (ii bis) is contained in III 3.3. Moreover, by virtue of 5.2 the conditions (i) and (ii) both imply that the \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) (\(i\leqslant n\)) are coherent. The equivalence of (i) and (ii) then follows from 5.4, taking into account the fact that for any coherent Module \(G\) on \(X\), one has \(G=0\) if and only if \(f_\ast(G(m))=0\) for \(m\) large, for example by virtue of EGA III 2.2.1 (iii).

Remark 5.6

One can interpret the criteria 5.2 and 5.5 by saying that the "simultaneous finiteness condition" 5.2 (ii) is expressed by properties of local regularity (in terms of depth thanks to VIII 2.1) of \(F\) at the points of \(U\) near \(Y=X- U\), whereas the "asymptotic vanishing condition" 5.5 (i) is of a distinctly stronger nature, and is expressed by conditions of local regularity of \(g_\ast(F)\) at the points of \(Y\) itself. It would be interesting, in order to generalize the Lefschetz-type theorems for projective morphisms to quasi-projective morphisms, to find necessary and sufficient local criteria on \(X\) for the \(\mathscr{S}\)-Modules \(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \operatorname{R}^ih_\ast(F(m))\) for \(i\leqslant n\) to be of finite type. When \(S\) is the spectrum of a field (and doubtless more generally, if it is the spectrum of an artinian ring) and \(Y=X- U\) is finite, one can show that it is necessary and sufficient that the following conditions be satisfied:

  1. 1)

    \(\operatorname{prof} F_x >n\) for every closed point \(x\) of \(U\) (compare 1.4).

  2. 2)

    \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) is coherent for \(i\leqslant n\), or what amounts to the same, there exists an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(Y\) such that for every closed point \(x\) of \(U\cap V\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} F_x >n+1\).

Proposition 5.7

Let \(S\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(g:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) a morphism of preschemes of finite type over \(S\)1, with structural morphisms \(h\) and \(f\), \(F\) a quasi-coherent Module on \(U\), \(n\) an integer. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. For every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), with \(S'\) noetherian, the module \(\operatorname{R}^ng'_\ast(F')\) on \(X'\) is coherent.

  2. For every base change as above, and every coherent Ideal \(\mathscr{J}\) on \(S'\), denoting by \(\mathscr{I}\) the Ideal \(\mathscr{J}\mathscr{O}_{X'}\) on \(X'\), the graded Module

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \operatorname{R}^ng'_\ast(\mathscr{I}^mF') \]

    over \(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{I}^m\) is of finite type.

  3. For every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), and \(\mathscr{J}\) as above, the graded Module

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \operatorname{R}^ng'_\ast(\mathscr{I}^mF'/\mathscr{I}^{m+1}F') \]

    over \(\operatorname{gr}_I(\mathscr{O}_{X'})=\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{I}^m/\mathscr{I}^{m+1}\) is of finite type.

Evidently (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) and (iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i), as one sees by taking \(\mathscr{I}=0\) in conditions (ii) and (iii). The converse implications are obtained by applying (i) to the composed base change \(S^{''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), where \(S^{''}\) is equal to \(\operatorname{Spec} \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{J}^m\) resp. \(\operatorname{Spec} \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{J}^m/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}\).

The interest of this proposition is that the conditions of form (ii) are those which intervene in the "algebraic-formal comparison theorems", whereas the conditions of form (iii) intervene in the "existence theorems" which complete them, cf. exposé IX. A first interesting case is that where \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is the identity, and where it is therefore a question of conditions on a morphism \(h:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) locally of finite type and a Module \(F\) quasi-coherent on \(U\) flat relative to \(S\). To obtain sufficient conditions, we shall suppose that \(U\) embeds by \(g:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), as an open sub-prescheme of an \(X\) proper over \(S\). Applying 5.1, one sees therefore:

Corollary 5.8

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper morphism, with \(S\) locally noetherian, \(U\) an open set of \(X\), \(g:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion, \(h=fg:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), \(F\) a quasi-coherent Module on \(X\), flat relative to \(S\). Suppose that for every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), with \(S'\) locally noetherian, one has \(\operatorname{R}^ig'_\ast(F')\) coherent on \(X'\) for \(i\leqslant n\). Then one has the following:

  1. For every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), with \(S'\) locally noetherian, \(\operatorname{R}^ih'_\ast(F')\) is coherent on \(S'\) for \(i\leqslant n\).

  2. For every \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) as above, and every coherent Ideal \(\mathscr{J}\) on \(S'\), the graded Modules

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \operatorname{R}^ih'_\ast(\mathscr{J}^mF') \]

    over \(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m\) are of finite type for \(i\leqslant n\).

  3. For every \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and \(\mathscr{J}\) as above, the graded Modules

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \operatorname{R}^ih'_\ast(\mathscr{J}^mF'/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}F') \]

    over \(\operatorname{gr}_\mathscr{J}(\mathscr{O}_{S'})=\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}\) are of finite type for \(i\leqslant n\).

    Moreover, under the conditions of (ii), and by virtue of the comparison theorem 1.1, denoting by \(\widehat{S'}\) the formal completion of \(S'\) with respect to \(\mathscr{J}\), and by \(\widehat{U'}\) that of \(U'\) with respect to \(\mathscr{J}\mathscr{O}_{U'}\), the canonical homomorphisms

    \[ \widehat{\operatorname{R}^ih'_\ast(F')} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{R}^i\widehat{h'}_\ast(\widehat{F'}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_k\operatorname{R}^ih'_\ast(F'_k) \]

    are isomorphisms for \(i\leqslant n-1\).

Remark 5.9

Suppose that one is moreover under the conditions of 5.8 with \(F\) coherent, and consider a base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) as in 5.9 (i). Suppose moreover that \(S'\) is locally immersible in a regular scheme, or more generally, that there exists a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism \(S^{''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), such that \(S^{''}\) is locally immersible in a regular scheme; this condition is satisfied in particular if \(S'\) is local. Then the conclusion of 5.8 (i) and (ii) remains valid when one replaces \(F'\) in it by a Module \(G'\) on \(U'\), such that every point of \(U'\) has an open neighbourhood on which \(G'\) is isomorphic to a Module of the form \({F'}^n\). Indeed, one is reduced to the case where \(S'\) itself is locally immersible in a regular scheme, so that the same holds for \(S^{''}=\operatorname{Spec}\big(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m\big)\) and for \(X^{''}=X'\times_{S'} S^{''}= X\times_{S} S^{''}\), which are of finite type over it. One then applies the finiteness criterion VIII 2.3 for the direct images for \(i\leqslant n\) of \(G^{''}\) under the immersion \(U^{''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^{''}\), noting that they are satisfied by hypothesis for \(F^{''}\), hence also for \(G^{''}\) since they are expressed in terms of depth and \(G^{''}\) is locally isomorphic to an \({F^{''}}^n\). The same argument shows that if \(\mathscr{G}'\) is a coherent Module on \(\widehat{U'}\) (completion of \(U'\) with respect to the Ideal \(\mathscr{J}\mathscr{O}_{U'}\)), such that \(\mathscr{G}'_0=\mathscr{G}'/\mathscr{J}\mathscr{G}'\) is locally of the form \(F_0^{'n}\), then the conclusion of (iii) remains valid by replacing \(F'\) in it by \(\mathscr{G}'\). One thus obtains the following result, using the results of exposé IX:

Corollary 5.10

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper morphism, with \(S\) locally noetherian, \(U\) an open part of \(X\); one supposes \(U\) flat relative to \(S\), and that for every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), with \(S'\) locally noetherian, one has \(R^ig'_\ast(\mathscr{O}_{U'})\) coherent on \(X'\) for \(i=0, 1\). Suppose then that \(S'\) is of the form \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), where \(A\) is a noetherian ring equipped with an ideal \(J\) such that \(A\) is separated and complete for the \(J\)-adic topology. Under these conditions:

  1. The functor \(F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\widehat{F}\) from the category of locally free Modules on \(U'\) to the category of locally free Modules on \(\widehat{U'}\) is fully faithful.

  2. For every locally free Module \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(\widehat{U'}\), there exists a coherent Module \(F\) on \(U'\) (not necessarily locally free, alas), and an isomorphism \(\widehat{F}\simeq \mathfrak{F}\).

It remains only to prove (ii), thanks to 5.9. Now according to this remark and 2.1 it follows that \(\mathfrak{F}\) is induced by a coherent Module \(\mathfrak{G}\) on \(\widehat{X'}\). According to the existence theorem EGA III 5.1.4, \(\mathfrak{G}\) is of the form \(\widehat{F}\), where \(F\) is coherent on \(X\), whence the conclusion.

Remarks 5.11

1o) Using 5.10, 4.7 and a suitable hypothesis, saying that certain local rings of the geometric fibres of \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) are "pure" resp. parafactorial, one should be able to obtain statements saying that the functor \(Z'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \widehat{Z'}\) from the category of étale coverings of \(X'\) to the category of étale coverings of \(\widehat{X'}\) (or what amounts to the same, of \(X_0'\)) is an equivalence of categories, resp. that the functor \(L\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \widehat{L}\) from the category of invertible Modules on \(X'\) to the category of invertible Modules on \(\widehat{X'}\) is an equivalence. Using recent results of Murre, it is probable that one should be able to deduce existence theorems for Picard schemes for certain non-proper algebraic schemes. In general, the elimination of purity hypotheses in various existence theorems, notably of representability of functors such as the Hilbert functors, or Picard functors etc., by means of the techniques developed in this seminar, deserves a systematic study.

2o) One may set oneself the task of giving workable necessary and sufficient conditions, in terms of depth, for the universal finiteness condition envisaged in 5.10 to be satisfied. When \(S\) is the spectrum of a field, it follows easily from EGA III 1.4.15 that it is necessary and sufficient that the \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) (\(i\leqslant n\)) be coherent, which is well expressed in terms of depth thanks to VIII 2.3. In the general case, one will note however that it is not sufficient to require that the preceding condition be satisfied for all the fibres \(U_s\subset X_s\) (\(s\in S\)), even in the case where \(n=0\). Take for example \(X=S\), \(S\) the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, \(U\) the open set reduced to the generic point, \(F=\mathscr{O}_U\).

3o) Here however is a sufficient condition ensuring that one is under the conditions of the hypothesis of 5.10: It suffices that \(f\) be flat, and that for every \(s\in S\) and every \(x\in Y_s=X_s- U_s\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X_s, x}\geqslant n+2. \]

Indeed, taking into account lemma 2.5 (cf. relation (16) after 2.5), it follows that one then has \(g_\ast(\mathscr{O}_U)\simeq\mathscr{O}_X\) and \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(\mathscr{O}_U)=0\) for \(0<i\leqslant n\), and the same relations will evidently be satisfied after every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\).

blocks.html: 5 Universal finiteness conditions for a non-proper morphism
Let us recall for the record the

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper morphism of preschemes, with \(S\) locally noetherian, \(U\) an open part of \(X\), \(g:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion, \(h=fg:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), \(F\) a Module on \(U\). Suppose that the Modules \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) are coherent for \(i\leqslant n\) (a hypothesis of local nature on \(X\), which is verified in practice by means of the criterion VIII 2.3). Then \(\operatorname{R}^ih_\ast(F)\) is coherent for \(i\leqslant n\).
This follows immediately from the Leray spectral sequence
\[ \tag{26} {} \operatorname{E}_2^{p, q}=\operatorname{R}^pf_\ast(\operatorname{R}^qg_\ast(F)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \operatorname{R}^\ast h_\ast(F), \]
and from the fact that the higher direct images under \(f\) of a coherent Module on \(X\) are coherent (EGA III 3.2.1).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2

Let \(S\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(\mathscr{S}\) a quasi-coherent graded Algebra, of finite type over \(S\), generated by \(\mathscr{S}_1\), \(X\) a sub-prescheme of \(\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S})\), \(\mathscr{O}_X(1)\) the invertible Module on \(X\) very ample relatively to \(S\) induced by \(\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S}(1))\), \(U\) an open part of \(X\), \(g:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion, \(h=fg:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), \(F\) a quasi-coherent Module on \(U\), whence twisted Modules \(F(m)=F\otimes \mathscr{O}_X(m)\)) (\(m\in\mathbf{Z}\)), \(n\) an integer, \(m_0\) an integer. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) is coherent for \(i\leqslant n\).

  2. \(\coprod_{m>m_0}\operatorname{R}^ih_\ast(F(m))\) is an \(\mathscr{S}\)-Module of finite type for \(i\leqslant n\).

blocks.title: Démonstration
blocks.html:
Proof▾

Replacing \(F\) by \(F(m)\) in the spectral sequence above, one finds a spectral sequence of graded \(\mathscr{S}\)-Modules

\[ \operatorname{E}_2^{p, q}= \coprod_{m\geqslant m_0} \operatorname{R}^pf_\ast(\operatorname{R}^qg_\ast(F(m)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \coprod_{m\geqslant m_0} \operatorname{R}^\ast h_\ast(F(m)). \]

Since one has

\[ \operatorname{R}^qg_\ast(F(m)) \simeq \operatorname{R}^qg_\ast(F) (m), \]

one sees that if the \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) are coherent, \(\operatorname{E}_2^{p, q}\) is of finite type over \(\mathscr{S}\) for \(q\leqslant n\), thanks to part a) of lemma 5.3 below, which implies that the abutment is of finite type over \(\mathscr{S}\) in degree \(i\leqslant n\). This proves (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Moreover, reasoning in the abelian category of graded \(\mathscr{S}\)-Modules modulo the thick subcategory \(\mathscr{C}\) of those which are quasi-coherent of finite type, one finds by the preceding spectral sequence

\[ \coprod_{m\geqslant m_0} \operatorname{R}^{n+1} h_\ast(F(m)) \simeq \coprod_{m\geqslant m_0} f_\ast (\operatorname{R}^{n+1} g_\ast(F)(m)) \mod \mathscr{C}, \]

which proves that if the left-hand side is an \(\mathscr{S}\)-Module of finite type, then \(\operatorname{R}^{n+1} g_\ast(F)\) is coherent, by virtue of part b) of lemma 5.3. This proves the implication (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) by induction on \(n\). It remains to prove:

Lemma 5.3

Let \(S\), \(\mathscr{S}\), \(X\), \(f\) be as in 5.2, and \(G\) a quasi-coherent Module on \(X\), \(m_0\) an integer. Then

  1. If \(G\) is coherent, then for every integer \(i\), the graded Module

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant m_0} \operatorname{R}^i f_\ast(G(m)) \]

    over \(\mathscr{S}\) is of finite type.

  2. Conversely, suppose that the Module \(\coprod_{m\geqslant m_0} \operatorname{R}^i f_\ast(G(m))\) over \(\mathscr{S}\) is of finite type, then \(G\) is coherent.

Proof of 5.3▾

For a), the case \(i=0\) is given in EGA III 2.3.2, the case \(i>0\) in EGA III 2.2.1 (i)(ii) which says that the \( \operatorname{R}^i f_\ast G(m)\) are coherent, and zero for \(m\) large (if one assumes \(S\) noetherian, which is permissible). For b), one notes that \(G\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Proj} (\coprod_{m\geqslant m_0}f_\ast(G(m))\) (EGA II 3.4.4 and 3.4.2), which proves that \(G\) is coherent if \(\coprod_{m\geqslant m_0}f_\ast(G(m))\) is of finite type over \(\mathscr{S}\), by virtue of loc. cit. 3.4.4.

Q.E.D.
Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.4 (\(S\) noetherian)

Suppose that \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) is coherent for \(i\leqslant n\), then for \(i\leqslant n+1\), and \(m\) large, one has a canonical isomorphism:

\[ \operatorname{R}^ih_\ast(F(m)) \simeq f_\ast(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(G)(m)). \]

Indeed the spectral sequence (26) for \(F(m)\) then degenerates in degree \(\leqslant n\), by EGA III 2.2.1 (ii), whence the result immediately (which moreover gives back the implication (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) of 5.2).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.5

Under the preliminary conditions of 5.2, \(S\) noetherian, the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\coprod_{m\geqslant m_0}h_\ast(F(m)\) is of finite type over \(S\), and \(\operatorname{R}^ih_\ast(F(m))=0\) for \(0<i\leqslant n\) and \(m\) large.

  2. \(g_\ast(F)\) is coherent, and \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)=0\) for \(0<i\leqslant n\).

  3. (ii bis)

    \(g_\ast(F)\) is coherent, and \(\operatorname{prof}_Y g_\ast(F)>n+1\).

The equivalence of (ii) and (ii bis) is contained in III 3.3. Moreover, by virtue of 5.2 the conditions (i) and (ii) both imply that the \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) (\(i\leqslant n\)) are coherent. The equivalence of (i) and (ii) then follows from 5.4, taking into account the fact that for any coherent Module \(G\) on \(X\), one has \(G=0\) if and only if \(f_\ast(G(m))=0\) for \(m\) large, for example by virtue of EGA III 2.2.1 (iii).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 5.6

One can interpret the criteria 5.2 and 5.5 by saying that the "simultaneous finiteness condition" 5.2 (ii) is expressed by properties of local regularity (in terms of depth thanks to VIII 2.1) of \(F\) at the points of \(U\) near \(Y=X- U\), whereas the "asymptotic vanishing condition" 5.5 (i) is of a distinctly stronger nature, and is expressed by conditions of local regularity of \(g_\ast(F)\) at the points of \(Y\) itself. It would be interesting, in order to generalize the Lefschetz-type theorems for projective morphisms to quasi-projective morphisms, to find necessary and sufficient local criteria on \(X\) for the \(\mathscr{S}\)-Modules \(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \operatorname{R}^ih_\ast(F(m))\) for \(i\leqslant n\) to be of finite type. When \(S\) is the spectrum of a field (and doubtless more generally, if it is the spectrum of an artinian ring) and \(Y=X- U\) is finite, one can show that it is necessary and sufficient that the following conditions be satisfied:

  1. 1)

    \(\operatorname{prof} F_x >n\) for every closed point \(x\) of \(U\) (compare 1.4).

  2. 2)

    \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) is coherent for \(i\leqslant n\), or what amounts to the same, there exists an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(Y\) such that for every closed point \(x\) of \(U\cap V\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} F_x >n+1\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.7

Let \(S\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(g:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) a morphism of preschemes of finite type over \(S\)1, with structural morphisms \(h\) and \(f\), \(F\) a quasi-coherent Module on \(U\), \(n\) an integer. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. For every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), with \(S'\) noetherian, the module \(\operatorname{R}^ng'_\ast(F')\) on \(X'\) is coherent.

  2. For every base change as above, and every coherent Ideal \(\mathscr{J}\) on \(S'\), denoting by \(\mathscr{I}\) the Ideal \(\mathscr{J}\mathscr{O}_{X'}\) on \(X'\), the graded Module

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \operatorname{R}^ng'_\ast(\mathscr{I}^mF') \]

    over \(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{I}^m\) is of finite type.

  3. For every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), and \(\mathscr{J}\) as above, the graded Module

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \operatorname{R}^ng'_\ast(\mathscr{I}^mF'/\mathscr{I}^{m+1}F') \]

    over \(\operatorname{gr}_I(\mathscr{O}_{X'})=\coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{I}^m/\mathscr{I}^{m+1}\) is of finite type.

Evidently (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) and (iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i), as one sees by taking \(\mathscr{I}=0\) in conditions (ii) and (iii). The converse implications are obtained by applying (i) to the composed base change \(S^{''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), where \(S^{''}\) is equal to \(\operatorname{Spec} \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{J}^m\) resp. \(\operatorname{Spec} \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \mathscr{J}^m/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}\).

The interest of this proposition is that the conditions of form (ii) are those which intervene in the "algebraic-formal comparison theorems", whereas the conditions of form (iii) intervene in the "existence theorems" which complete them, cf. exposé IX. A first interesting case is that where \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is the identity, and where it is therefore a question of conditions on a morphism \(h:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) locally of finite type and a Module \(F\) quasi-coherent on \(U\) flat relative to \(S\). To obtain sufficient conditions, we shall suppose that \(U\) embeds by \(g:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), as an open sub-prescheme of an \(X\) proper over \(S\). Applying 5.1, one sees therefore:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.8

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper morphism, with \(S\) locally noetherian, \(U\) an open set of \(X\), \(g:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion, \(h=fg:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), \(F\) a quasi-coherent Module on \(X\), flat relative to \(S\). Suppose that for every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), with \(S'\) locally noetherian, one has \(\operatorname{R}^ig'_\ast(F')\) coherent on \(X'\) for \(i\leqslant n\). Then one has the following:

  1. For every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), with \(S'\) locally noetherian, \(\operatorname{R}^ih'_\ast(F')\) is coherent on \(S'\) for \(i\leqslant n\).

  2. For every \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) as above, and every coherent Ideal \(\mathscr{J}\) on \(S'\), the graded Modules

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \operatorname{R}^ih'_\ast(\mathscr{J}^mF') \]

    over \(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m\) are of finite type for \(i\leqslant n\).

  3. For every \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and \(\mathscr{J}\) as above, the graded Modules

    \[ \coprod_{m\geqslant 0} \operatorname{R}^ih'_\ast(\mathscr{J}^mF'/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}F') \]

    over \(\operatorname{gr}_\mathscr{J}(\mathscr{O}_{S'})=\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m/\mathscr{J}^{m+1}\) are of finite type for \(i\leqslant n\).

    Moreover, under the conditions of (ii), and by virtue of the comparison theorem 1.1, denoting by \(\widehat{S'}\) the formal completion of \(S'\) with respect to \(\mathscr{J}\), and by \(\widehat{U'}\) that of \(U'\) with respect to \(\mathscr{J}\mathscr{O}_{U'}\), the canonical homomorphisms

    \[ \widehat{\operatorname{R}^ih'_\ast(F')} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{R}^i\widehat{h'}_\ast(\widehat{F'}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_k\operatorname{R}^ih'_\ast(F'_k) \]

    are isomorphisms for \(i\leqslant n-1\).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 5.9

Suppose that one is moreover under the conditions of 5.8 with \(F\) coherent, and consider a base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) as in 5.9 (i). Suppose moreover that \(S'\) is locally immersible in a regular scheme, or more generally, that there exists a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism \(S^{''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), such that \(S^{''}\) is locally immersible in a regular scheme; this condition is satisfied in particular if \(S'\) is local. Then the conclusion of 5.8 (i) and (ii) remains valid when one replaces \(F'\) in it by a Module \(G'\) on \(U'\), such that every point of \(U'\) has an open neighbourhood on which \(G'\) is isomorphic to a Module of the form \({F'}^n\). Indeed, one is reduced to the case where \(S'\) itself is locally immersible in a regular scheme, so that the same holds for \(S^{''}=\operatorname{Spec}\big(\coprod_{m\geqslant 0}\mathscr{J}^m\big)\) and for \(X^{''}=X'\times_{S'} S^{''}= X\times_{S} S^{''}\), which are of finite type over it. One then applies the finiteness criterion VIII 2.3 for the direct images for \(i\leqslant n\) of \(G^{''}\) under the immersion \(U^{''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^{''}\), noting that they are satisfied by hypothesis for \(F^{''}\), hence also for \(G^{''}\) since they are expressed in terms of depth and \(G^{''}\) is locally isomorphic to an \({F^{''}}^n\). The same argument shows that if \(\mathscr{G}'\) is a coherent Module on \(\widehat{U'}\) (completion of \(U'\) with respect to the Ideal \(\mathscr{J}\mathscr{O}_{U'}\)), such that \(\mathscr{G}'_0=\mathscr{G}'/\mathscr{J}\mathscr{G}'\) is locally of the form \(F_0^{'n}\), then the conclusion of (iii) remains valid by replacing \(F'\) in it by \(\mathscr{G}'\). One thus obtains the following result, using the results of exposé IX:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.10

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper morphism, with \(S\) locally noetherian, \(U\) an open part of \(X\); one supposes \(U\) flat relative to \(S\), and that for every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), with \(S'\) locally noetherian, one has \(R^ig'_\ast(\mathscr{O}_{U'})\) coherent on \(X'\) for \(i=0, 1\). Suppose then that \(S'\) is of the form \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), where \(A\) is a noetherian ring equipped with an ideal \(J\) such that \(A\) is separated and complete for the \(J\)-adic topology. Under these conditions:

  1. The functor \(F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\widehat{F}\) from the category of locally free Modules on \(U'\) to the category of locally free Modules on \(\widehat{U'}\) is fully faithful.

  2. For every locally free Module \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(\widehat{U'}\), there exists a coherent Module \(F\) on \(U'\) (not necessarily locally free, alas), and an isomorphism \(\widehat{F}\simeq \mathfrak{F}\).

It remains only to prove (ii), thanks to 5.9. Now according to this remark and 2.1 it follows that \(\mathfrak{F}\) is induced by a coherent Module \(\mathfrak{G}\) on \(\widehat{X'}\). According to the existence theorem EGA III 5.1.4, \(\mathfrak{G}\) is of the form \(\widehat{F}\), where \(F\) is coherent on \(X\), whence the conclusion.

blocks.title: Remarques
blocks.html:
Remarks 5.11

1o) Using 5.10, 4.7 and a suitable hypothesis, saying that certain local rings of the geometric fibres of \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) are "pure" resp. parafactorial, one should be able to obtain statements saying that the functor \(Z'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \widehat{Z'}\) from the category of étale coverings of \(X'\) to the category of étale coverings of \(\widehat{X'}\) (or what amounts to the same, of \(X_0'\)) is an equivalence of categories, resp. that the functor \(L\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \widehat{L}\) from the category of invertible Modules on \(X'\) to the category of invertible Modules on \(\widehat{X'}\) is an equivalence. Using recent results of Murre, it is probable that one should be able to deduce existence theorems for Picard schemes for certain non-proper algebraic schemes. In general, the elimination of purity hypotheses in various existence theorems, notably of representability of functors such as the Hilbert functors, or Picard functors etc., by means of the techniques developed in this seminar, deserves a systematic study.

2o) One may set oneself the task of giving workable necessary and sufficient conditions, in terms of depth, for the universal finiteness condition envisaged in 5.10 to be satisfied. When \(S\) is the spectrum of a field, it follows easily from EGA III 1.4.15 that it is necessary and sufficient that the \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(F)\) (\(i\leqslant n\)) be coherent, which is well expressed in terms of depth thanks to VIII 2.3. In the general case, one will note however that it is not sufficient to require that the preceding condition be satisfied for all the fibres \(U_s\subset X_s\) (\(s\in S\)), even in the case where \(n=0\). Take for example \(X=S\), \(S\) the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, \(U\) the open set reduced to the generic point, \(F=\mathscr{O}_U\).

3o) Here however is a sufficient condition ensuring that one is under the conditions of the hypothesis of 5.10: It suffices that \(f\) be flat, and that for every \(s\in S\) and every \(x\in Y_s=X_s- U_s\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X_s, x}\geqslant n+2. \]

Indeed, taking into account lemma 2.5 (cf. relation (16) after 2.5), it follows that one then has \(g_\ast(\mathscr{O}_U)\simeq\mathscr{O}_X\) and \(\operatorname{R}^ig_\ast(\mathscr{O}_U)=0\) for \(0<i\leqslant n\), and the same relations will evidently be satisfied after every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\).

footnotes.html: It in fact suffices that \(g\) be quasi-compact and quasi-separated (EGA IV1.2.1), without condition on \(U, X\).
